questions.question: What is the difference between INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL OUTER JOIN?
questions.answer:
JOIN types control which rows survive when two tables are combined:

  • INNER JOIN — only rows where the condition matches in both tables. Most common in day-to-day queries.
  • LEFT JOIN — all rows from the left table + matching rows from the right. Non-matching right rows become NULL. I use this when I need "all orders including those with no payment yet".
  • RIGHT JOIN — mirror of LEFT JOIN. Rarely write this; I just flip the table order and use LEFT JOIN for clarity.
  • FULL OUTER JOIN — all rows from both sides, NULLs where there's no match. Useful for reconciliation queries — "show me everything on both sides that doesn't match".

In FolioTrack I had a LEFT JOIN between Portfolios and PortfolioHoldings to list all portfolios even if they had zero holdings yet — INNER JOIN would have excluded empty portfolios.
followUps: What's a CROSS JOIN?
When would you use a self-join?
How does a JOIN affect query performance?

followUpAnswers.What's a CROSS JOIN?:
A CROSS JOIN produces the Cartesian product of two tables — every row from the left table paired with every row from the right table. If table A has 5 rows and table B has 10 rows, the result has 50 rows.

-- Generate all combinations of sizes and colours
SELECT s.SizeName, c.ColourName
FROM Sizes s
CROSS JOIN Colours c;

When is it useful?

  • Generating combinations: all product variants (size × colour), all date × category slots for a pivot report.
  • Populating a calendar table: cross join a numbers table to generate every day of the year.
  • Test data generation: produce large synthetic datasets quickly.

Warning: CROSS JOIN on two large tables with no filter will produce billions of rows. Always double-check you actually intend a Cartesian product — accidental CROSS JOINs (forgetting the ON clause in older implicit join syntax) are a classic performance disaster.

No ON clause is required — the join condition is the product itself. You can add a WHERE clause to filter the result afterwards.
followUpAnswers.When would you use a self-join?:
A self-join joins a table to itself, treating the same table as two different logical tables using aliases. The classic use case is hierarchical data stored in a single table.

-- Employee hierarchy: find each employee and their manager
SELECT
    e.Name        AS Employee,
    m.Name        AS Manager
FROM Employees e
LEFT JOIN Employees m ON e.ManagerId = m.Id;

-- Find pairs of employees in the same department
SELECT a.Name AS Emp1, b.Name AS Emp2, a.DepartmentId
FROM Employees a
INNER JOIN Employees b
    ON a.DepartmentId = b.DepartmentId
    AND a.Id < b.Id;  -- avoid (A,B) and (B,A) duplicates

Common self-join scenarios:

  • Employee → Manager relationships (org chart)
  • Category → Parent Category (threaded comments, product categories)
  • Finding duplicate rows with slightly different IDs
  • Comparing a row to other rows in the same table (e.g., comparing a sale to the previous day's sale)

For deep hierarchies (more than 2–3 levels), a recursive CTE is usually cleaner than chaining multiple self-joins.
followUpAnswers.How does a JOIN affect query performance:
Every JOIN adds work for the query engine. SQL Server has three physical join algorithms it can choose from:

  • Nested Loop Join — for each row in the outer table, scan the inner table. O(n×m). Fast when the outer result is small and the inner table has an index. Most efficient join type when used correctly.
  • Hash Join — builds a hash table from the smaller input, probes it with the larger input. O(n+m) but requires memory. Used when no useful index exists on the join column. Can spill to tempdb if memory is insufficient.
  • Merge Join — requires both inputs sorted on the join key. Very fast when both sides are already ordered (e.g., both are clustered index scans). O(n+m).

Performance killers with JOINs:

  • No index on the join column — forces hash or full scan nested loop.
  • JOINing on a computed expression: ON YEAR(a.CreatedAt) = YEAR(b.CreatedAt) — function prevents index use.
  • JOINing on mismatched data types — causes implicit conversion, disables index seeks.
  • Too many JOINs — optimizer has exponential plan space to search; past 8–10 tables it may time out and pick a suboptimal plan.

The single best thing you can do: ensure the JOIN column on both sides has an index. Check the execution plan for hash joins on large tables — that's the signal to add an index.

questions.tip: Interview gotcha: If asked "what's the default JOIN?", the answer is INNER JOIN. Also be ready to sketch a Venn diagram — interviewers love that.

questions.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
questions.answer:
Simple rule I follow: WHERE filters rows before grouping, HAVING filters groups after grouping.

WHERE works on individual row data — you can't use aggregate functions like SUM() or COUNT() in a WHERE clause. HAVING works on the result of a GROUP BY and can reference aggregates.

-- WHERE: filter rows before grouping
SELECT CategoryId, SUM(Amount) AS Total
FROM Orders
WHERE Status = 'Completed'  -- filters individual rows
GROUP BY CategoryId
HAVING SUM(Amount) > 10000; -- filters aggregated groups

Real example: in the PaymentReminder service I needed "categories that have more than 5 overdue invoices". That's a HAVING query — you can't know the count until after grouping.
followUps: Can you use column alias in HAVING?
What's the execution order of SQL clauses?

followUpAnswers.Can you use column alias in HAVING?:
In standard SQL and SQL Server: no, you cannot use a SELECT-defined alias in HAVING. The reason is SQL's logical execution order — HAVING is evaluated before SELECT, so the alias doesn't exist yet when HAVING runs.

-- This FAILS in SQL Server:
SELECT ClientId, SUM(Amount) AS TotalAmount
FROM Invoices
GROUP BY ClientId
HAVING TotalAmount > 10000;  -- ERROR: Invalid column name 'TotalAmount'

-- Correct: repeat the expression
SELECT ClientId, SUM(Amount) AS TotalAmount
FROM Invoices
GROUP BY ClientId
HAVING SUM(Amount) > 10000;  -- works

-- Alternative: wrap in a subquery/CTE
WITH Summary AS (
    SELECT ClientId, SUM(Amount) AS TotalAmount
    FROM Invoices
    GROUP BY ClientId
)
SELECT * FROM Summary WHERE TotalAmount > 10000;

Note: Some databases like MySQL and PostgreSQL are more permissive and do allow aliases in HAVING as an extension to the standard. SQL Server strictly follows the standard. When in doubt, repeat the expression or wrap in a CTE.
followUpAnswers.What's the execution order of SQL clause:
SQL's logical execution order is different from the written order. This explains many confusing behaviours (why you can't use SELECT aliases in WHERE, why aggregate functions aren't allowed in WHERE, etc.):

  1. FROM — identify the source tables
  2. JOIN / ON — apply join conditions, produce combined rows
  3. WHERE — filter individual rows (no aggregates allowed here)
  4. GROUP BY — collapse rows into groups
  5. HAVING — filter groups (aggregates allowed here)
  6. SELECT — compute output columns, apply aliases
  7. DISTINCT — remove duplicate rows from result
  8. ORDER BY — sort (SELECT aliases ARE available here because SELECT ran first)
  9. OFFSET / FETCH (TOP) — pagination/limit

Practical implications:

  • Can't use SUM() in WHERE → use HAVING instead
  • Can't use SELECT alias in WHERE or HAVING → repeat the expression
  • CAN use SELECT alias in ORDER BY (ORDER BY runs after SELECT)
  • WHERE filters before GROUP BY → more efficient than HAVING for row-level filters

Memorising this order resolves about 80% of "why doesn't this SQL work?" puzzles.

questions.tip: SQL execution order: FROM → JOIN → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Memorise this — it explains why you can't use SELECT aliases in WHERE.

questions.question: What is an index and how does it improve query performance?
questions.answer:
An index is a separate data structure (typically a B-tree) that SQL Server maintains alongside a table. Instead of scanning every row (full table scan), the engine can jump to the matching rows using the index — like a book index vs reading every page.

Clustered index — determines the physical storage order of the table. One per table. By default, SQL Server creates it on the primary key.

Non-clustered index — a separate structure with a pointer back to the data row. You can have many per table. Add these on columns you filter or join on frequently.

In the CSInvoice project, a query on Invoices WHERE ClientId = @id AND Status = 'Pending' was doing a full scan of 200k rows. Adding a composite non-clustered index on (ClientId, Status) dropped it from 3 seconds to 40ms.

Trade-off: Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE have to update the index too). Don't over-index — only add indexes for actual slow queries.

projectExample.explanation: Composite index on filter columns; INCLUDE adds frequently SELECTed columns as covering index to avoid key lookups.

followUps: What is a covering index?
What is index fragmentation?
When would you drop an index?

followUpAnswers.What is a covering index?:
A covering index is an index that contains all the columns a query needs — so SQL Server can satisfy the entire query from the index alone without going back to the main table (no key lookup).

-- Query that needs ClientId, Status, Amount, InvoiceDate
SELECT ClientId, Status, Amount, InvoiceDate
FROM Invoices
WHERE ClientId = 42 AND Status = 'Pending';

-- Non-covering index: seek in index, then key lookup for Amount+InvoiceDate
CREATE INDEX IX_Basic ON Invoices (ClientId, Status);

-- Covering index: all needed columns in the index, zero key lookups
CREATE INDEX IX_Covering ON Invoices (ClientId, Status)
INCLUDE (Amount, InvoiceDate);

The INCLUDE clause adds columns to the leaf level of the index without including them in the sort key. This means:

  • Columns in INCLUDE don't affect the sort order or index seek efficiency
  • They're stored at the leaf level, eliminating the round-trip to the clustered index (key lookup)
  • Key lookups show up in execution plans as a separate operator — they're expensive at scale because each one is a random I/O

Covering indexes are the single highest-impact index optimization I've applied. In CSInvoice, converting a non-covering index to a covering index on the Invoices query eliminated thousands of key lookups per second.
followUpAnswers.What is index fragmentation?:
Index fragmentation occurs when the logical order of pages in an index doesn't match the physical order on disk, or when pages are partially filled with wasted space. It happens naturally as data is inserted, updated, and deleted over time.

Two types:

  • Internal fragmentation (page density) — pages are only partially full (e.g., 40% used). Wastes I/O because you read a page to get few rows. Caused by deletes or inserts with FILL FACTOR headroom.
  • External fragmentation (logical scan fragmentation) — the next logical page is not the next physical page. Hurts sequential scan performance because the disk head has to jump around.
-- Check fragmentation level
SELECT
    OBJECT_NAME(ips.object_id) AS TableName,
    i.name AS IndexName,
    ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent,
    ips.page_count
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(DB_ID(), NULL, NULL, NULL, 'LIMITED') ips
JOIN sys.indexes i ON ips.object_id = i.object_id AND ips.index_id = i.index_id
ORDER BY ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent DESC;

Fix: Under 10% fragmentation — leave it. 10–30% — ALTER INDEX REBUILD or REORGANIZE. Over 30% — ALTER INDEX REBUILD (offline, rebuilds entire index). Schedule index maintenance jobs weekly on busy OLTP databases.
followUpAnswers.When would you drop an index?:
Indexes cost write performance and storage. You should drop an index when:

  • It's unused — SQL Server tracks index usage in sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats. If user_seeks + user_scans + user_lookups = 0 since last restart, it's never being used for reads but is still being updated on every write.
  • It's redundant — if you have indexes on (A) and (A, B), the single-column index is redundant for queries that also use B, and may confuse the optimizer.
  • Write performance is suffering — tables with 20+ indexes where inserts are taking too long. Each index is an additional write on every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE.
  • It's a duplicate — identical or near-identical indexes created by different developers over time.
-- Find unused indexes (reset after server restart, wait at least a week)
SELECT
    OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id) AS TableName,
    i.name AS IndexName,
    i.type_desc,
    ISNULL(s.user_seeks, 0) + ISNULL(s.user_scans, 0) AS TotalReads,
    ISNULL(s.user_updates, 0) AS TotalWrites
FROM sys.indexes i
LEFT JOIN sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats s
    ON i.object_id = s.object_id AND i.index_id = s.index_id AND s.database_id = DB_ID()
WHERE OBJECTPROPERTY(i.object_id, 'IsUserTable') = 1
    AND i.type_desc != 'HEAP'
ORDER BY TotalReads ASC;

Never drop an index without monitoring the query workload for a week first — usage stats reset on restart and some indexes are used only by monthly batch jobs.

questions.tip: Covering index = index includes all columns the query needs so SQL Server never has to go back to the table (eliminates key lookup). This is the single biggest performance win I've gotten in production.

questions.question: What is a stored procedure and what are its advantages over inline SQL?
questions.answer:
A stored procedure is a named, precompiled block of SQL stored in the database. You call it by name with parameters rather than sending raw SQL from application code.

Advantages:

  • Execution plan caching — SQL Server compiles and caches the plan on first call. Subsequent calls reuse it, avoiding recompilation overhead.
  • Security — you can grant EXECUTE permission on the proc without exposing the underlying tables at all.
  • Network efficiency — send one short EXEC command instead of a multi-KB SQL string.
  • Centralised logic — business rules live in DB, consistent regardless of which app calls it.

Disadvantages: Hard to version control, logic scattered between app and DB, painful to unit test. In the offline invoice project we had a stored proc for the nightly reconciliation job (Dapper + CommandType.StoredProcedure) but all read queries stayed as parameterized SQL in the application.
followUps: What is sp_executesql?
What is parameter sniffing?
Stored procedure vs view vs function?

followUpAnswers.What is sp_executesql?:
sp_executesql is a system stored procedure that executes a dynamic SQL string with parameters. It's the safe way to build dynamic SQL when the query shape must change at runtime.

-- BAD: string concatenation (injection risk, no plan reuse)
DECLARE @sql NVARCHAR(500) = 'SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = ' + @clientId;
EXEC(@sql);

-- GOOD: sp_executesql with parameters
DECLARE @sql NVARCHAR(500) = N'SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = @cid';
DECLARE @params NVARCHAR(100) = N'@cid INT';
EXEC sp_executesql @sql, @params, @cid = @clientId;

Why sp_executesql over EXEC(@sql):

  • Plan reuse — parameterized queries produce a stable plan cache entry. The same SQL text with different @cid values reuses the same plan. EXEC() with concatenated values produces a new plan entry per value.
  • SQL injection prevention — parameters are never interpreted as SQL, even if they contain single quotes or semicolons.
  • Type safety — you declare parameter types explicitly in the @params declaration.

Use sp_executesql when you need dynamic column names, dynamic ORDER BY, or conditional WHERE clauses that change based on application input.
followUpAnswers.What is parameter sniffing?:
Parameter sniffing is when SQL Server compiles a stored procedure's execution plan using the first set of parameter values it sees, then caches that plan for all future calls — even when later calls use very different parameter values that would benefit from a different plan.

Classic problem scenario:

CREATE PROCEDURE GetInvoicesByClient @ClientId INT AS
    SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = @ClientId;

-- First call: ClientId = 999 (1 invoice) → optimizer builds index seek plan
-- Second call: ClientId = 1 (500,000 invoices) → reuses seek plan, should be table scan
-- Result: terrible performance for large clients

Solutions:

  • OPTION (RECOMPILE) — recompile every execution. Eliminates sniffing but adds CPU cost per call. Good for infrequently called procs with wildly varying parameters.
  • OPTION (OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN) — compile with average statistics rather than the sniffed value. More balanced plan.
  • OPTION (OPTIMIZE FOR (@ClientId = 42)) — optimize for a specific representative value.
  • Use local variables — assigning params to local variables before use breaks sniffing (optimizer uses statistics instead of the sniffed value).
-- Local variable workaround
CREATE PROCEDURE GetInvoicesByClient @ClientId INT AS
BEGIN
    DECLARE @Id INT = @ClientId;  -- breaks sniffing
    SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = @Id;
END
followUpAnswers.Stored procedure vs view vs function?:
These three objects serve different purposes and the choice matters:

FeatureStored ProcedureViewFunction (TVF/Scalar)
ReturnsResult sets, output params, return codeVirtual table (rows/cols)Scalar value or table
Can be used in SELECTNoYesYes
Can modify dataYes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)Limited (simple views only)No (functions are side-effect free)
ParametersInput + output paramsNo parametersInput params only
TransactionsFull transaction controlNoNo
PerformancePlan cached, best for complex logicInlined by optimizerScalar functions can be very slow (per-row invocation)

Decision rules:

  • Use a stored procedure when you need to modify data, use output parameters, manage transactions, or run complex multi-step logic.
  • Use a view for reusable SELECT logic, security abstraction, or backward-compatible table renaming.
  • Use an inline TVF (table-valued function) when you need a parameterized view — it's inlined by the optimizer like a view but accepts parameters. Avoid scalar functions in WHERE clauses — they execute once per row and kill performance.

questions.tip: Parameter sniffing — SQL Server optimises the execution plan for the first parameter values it sees. If you later call with very different values (e.g., a customer with 1 order vs 100,000 orders), the cached plan may be terrible. Fix: OPTION(RECOMPILE) or OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN.

questions.question: What are SQL transactions and what are ACID properties?
questions.answer:
A transaction is a unit of work that either fully completes or fully rolls back — no partial state. ACID is the guarantee set:

  • Atomicity — all operations succeed or all roll back. Transfer ₹500: debit one account AND credit another as one atomic unit.
  • Consistency — DB moves from one valid state to another. Constraints, foreign keys, triggers all enforce this.
  • Isolation — concurrent transactions don't see each other's partial work. Controlled by isolation level.
  • Durability — committed data survives crashes. SQL Server writes to transaction log first (write-ahead logging).

In the WalletVerification project, a user's wallet top-up involved inserting a WalletTransaction record AND updating the Wallet.Balance. Without a transaction, a crash mid-way would debit the user without crediting the balance. We wrapped both in BEGIN TRANSACTION...COMMIT (and in EF Core via dbContext.Database.BeginTransactionAsync()).
followUps: What are SQL isolation levels?
What is a deadlock?
Explain dirty read, phantom read, non-repeatable read

followUpAnswers.What are SQL isolation levels?:
SQL isolation levels control how much one transaction can see from other concurrent transactions. SQL Server supports five levels:

  • READ UNCOMMITTED — reads dirty (uncommitted) data from other transactions. Fastest but dangerous. Only useful for approximate reporting where precision isn't critical.
  • READ COMMITTED (default) — only reads committed data. Acquires and immediately releases shared locks on each row read. Prevents dirty reads but not non-repeatable reads or phantoms.
  • REPEATABLE READ — holds shared locks until transaction ends, preventing other transactions from modifying rows you've read. Prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads but not phantom reads.
  • SERIALIZABLE — highest isolation. Range locks prevent other transactions from inserting rows that would match your query. Prevents all anomalies but highest contention.
  • SNAPSHOT — MVCC-based. Readers see a consistent snapshot of the DB from when their transaction started. No read locks, no blocking. Prevents all anomalies without the contention of SERIALIZABLE.
-- Set isolation level for current session
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SNAPSHOT;
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    SELECT Balance FROM Wallets WHERE UserId = @uid;
    -- other operations
COMMIT;
followUpAnswers.What is a deadlock?:
A deadlock occurs when two or more transactions each hold a lock that the other needs, and neither can proceed. SQL Server's lock monitor detects this cycle (typically within 5 seconds), picks the cheapest transaction as the deadlock victim, rolls it back, and throws error 1205 to the victim's connection.

-- Transaction A                    -- Transaction B
BEGIN TRAN                          BEGIN TRAN
UPDATE Accounts SET ... WHERE Id=1  UPDATE Accounts SET ... WHERE Id=2
-- holds lock on row 1              -- holds lock on row 2
UPDATE Accounts SET ... WHERE Id=2  UPDATE Accounts SET ... WHERE Id=1
-- waits for B's lock on row 2      -- waits for A's lock on row 1
-- DEADLOCK DETECTED

How to prevent deadlocks:

  • Access tables and rows in the same order across all transactions
  • Keep transactions short — reduce lock hold time
  • Use READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT (MVCC) — readers don't take shared locks, eliminating reader-writer deadlocks
  • Add proper indexes — table scans lock far more rows than index seeks, increasing collision probability
  • Avoid user interaction mid-transaction — never hold a transaction open while waiting for user input
followUpAnswers.Explain dirty read, phantom read, non-re:
These are the three classic concurrency anomalies that isolation levels protect against:

  • Dirty Read — Transaction A reads data that Transaction B has modified but not yet committed. If B rolls back, A has read data that never existed.
    Example: A reads a wallet balance that B is mid-update on. B rolls back. A used a phantom balance.
    Prevented by: READ COMMITTED and above.
  • Non-Repeatable Read — Transaction A reads a row, Transaction B updates and commits it, A reads the same row again and gets a different value within the same transaction.
    Example: A reads stock quantity = 10 at start of transaction. B sells 3 units and commits. A reads quantity again = 7. Same transaction, different values.
    Prevented by: REPEATABLE READ and above.
  • Phantom Read — Transaction A runs a range query (e.g., WHERE salary > 50000), Transaction B inserts a new row matching that range and commits, A runs the same query and gets a different set of rows.
    Example: A counts invoices due today = 5. B inserts a new invoice due today. A re-counts = 6. A never modified anything — new rows "appeared".
    Prevented by: SERIALIZABLE and SNAPSHOT.
AnomalyRCRRSerializableSnapshot
Dirty Read✗✗✗✗
Non-Repeatable Read✓✗✗✗
Phantom Read✓✓✗✗

questions.tip: Isolation levels ranked low→high: READ UNCOMMITTED → READ COMMITTED (SQL Server default) → REPEATABLE READ → SERIALIZABLE. Higher isolation = fewer anomalies but more locking/blocking. SNAPSHOT isolation is a great middle ground — MVCC-based, no read locks.

questions.question: What are SQL isolation levels and what concurrency problems do they solve?
questions.answer:
Isolation levels control what a transaction can see from other in-progress transactions. The three anomalies they protect against:

  • Dirty read — reading uncommitted data from another transaction that may roll back.
  • Non-repeatable read — re-reading the same row in the same transaction and getting a different value because another transaction committed in between.
  • Phantom read — re-running a range query and seeing new rows appear (another transaction inserted and committed).
LevelDirty ReadNon-RepeatablePhantom
READ UNCOMMITTED✓ possible✓✓
READ COMMITTED✗ prevented✓✓
REPEATABLE READ✗✗✓
SERIALIZABLE✗✗✗

In WalletVerification we explicitly used READ COMMITTED (SQL Server default) for most queries and SERIALIZABLE only for the debit/credit pair to prevent double-spend.
followUps: What is SNAPSHOT isolation?
How does SQL Server implement READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT?

followUpAnswers.What is SNAPSHOT isolation?:
SNAPSHOT isolation is an MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) based isolation level. Instead of blocking readers with shared locks, SQL Server maintains versioned copies of rows in tempdb (the version store). Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot of the database as it existed when the transaction started.

Key characteristics:

  • Readers never block writers, writers never block readers — the core benefit. Massive improvement in concurrency vs lock-based isolation.
  • Writers still block other writers (they still need update locks).
  • Provides the same anomaly prevention as SERIALIZABLE (no dirty, non-repeatable, or phantom reads).
  • Uses tempdb for the version store — high write workloads increase tempdb usage.
-- Enable SNAPSHOT isolation on a database
ALTER DATABASE MyDatabase SET ALLOW_SNAPSHOT_ISOLATION ON;

-- Use in a session
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SNAPSHOT;
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = @id;  -- sees snapshot from BEGIN TRAN
COMMIT;

When to use: Reporting queries that run long and shouldn't be blocked by OLTP writes. Long-running transactions that need a consistent view. Azure SQL has SNAPSHOT available by default.
followUpAnswers.How does SQL Server implement READ COMMI:
READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT Isolation (RCSI) is a database-level setting that changes how the default READ COMMITTED isolation level works — from lock-based to MVCC-based. It's the most impactful concurrency setting for most OLTP applications.

-- Enable RCSI (requires exclusive access to the database briefly)
ALTER DATABASE MyDatabase SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON;

How it works:

  • Every modified row stores the previous version in tempdb's version store with a transaction sequence number (XSN).
  • When a reader (under READ COMMITTED) reads a row being modified by another transaction, it reads the last committed version from the version store instead of waiting for a lock.
  • The reader gets the last committed value — no dirty reads, no blocking.

RCSI vs SNAPSHOT isolation:

  • RCSI: each statement sees the last committed version at the start of that statement. READ COMMITTED behaviour, just without locks.
  • SNAPSHOT: each transaction sees the version as of the transaction start. Full snapshot semantics.

Azure SQL enables RCSI by default. For most OLTP applications, simply enabling RCSI on SQL Server eliminates the majority of blocking and timeout issues without any code changes. This was the single biggest win in the invoice project — enabled RCSI and blocking complaints disappeared overnight.

questions.tip: SNAPSHOT isolation (MVCC) lets readers not block writers and vice versa. Enable with ALTER DATABASE SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON. Azure SQL has it on by default. This eliminates most blocking in OLTP systems.

questions.question: What is normalisation and what are the normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)?
questions.answer:
Normalisation is the process of structuring a database to reduce data redundancy and improve integrity.

  • 1NF (First Normal Form) — each column holds atomic (single) values; no repeating groups. No array-of-emails in one cell.
  • 2NF (Second Normal Form) — must be in 1NF + every non-key column depends on the whole primary key (no partial dependency). Applies when you have composite PKs.
  • 3NF (Third Normal Form) — must be in 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key column depends only on the PK, not on another non-key column). E.g., storing PostalCode AND City when City is determined by PostalCode — extract to a PostalCodes table.

In FolioTrack I had a denormalized Holdings table that stored StockSymbol and StockName together. When a stock name changed I had to update thousands of rows. Splitting into a Stocks table (3NF) fixed it.
followUps: What is BCNF?
When would you deliberately denormalize?

followUpAnswers.What is BCNF?:
Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) is a slightly stronger version of 3NF. A table is in BCNF if for every functional dependency X → Y, X is a superkey (a set of columns that can uniquely identify a row). In practice, 3NF and BCNF differ only in rare edge cases involving multiple overlapping candidate keys.

Example where 3NF holds but BCNF doesn't:

-- Table: StudentCourseTeacher (StudentId, CourseId, TeacherId)
-- Assumption: each teacher teaches only one course, each course has multiple teachers
-- FDs: (StudentId, CourseId) → TeacherId  (candidate key)
--      TeacherId → CourseId              (TeacherId is NOT a superkey, but determines CourseId)
-- This is 3NF (no transitive non-key deps) but NOT BCNF (TeacherId → CourseId violates BCNF)

Fix for BCNF violation: decompose into two tables — TeacherCourse(TeacherId, CourseId) and StudentTeacher(StudentId, TeacherId).

Practical note: In day-to-day database design, 3NF is the practical target. BCNF is mentioned in interviews to demonstrate depth. Real-world schemas rarely have the overlapping candidate key scenario that distinguishes BCNF from 3NF.
followUpAnswers.When would you deliberately denormalize?:
Denormalization trades data redundancy for query performance. It's a deliberate design decision, not an accident. Valid scenarios:

  • Reporting and analytics (OLAP) — complex reports joining 8 normalized tables are slow. A pre-joined summary table (denormalized) reads far fewer pages. Data warehouses use star schemas (fact table + dimension tables) which are intentionally denormalized.
  • Computed aggregates — storing Order.TotalAmount as a column instead of summing OrderItems on every read. Maintain it via trigger or application logic on write.
  • Avoiding joins in hot paths — if a frequently-executed query joins 5 tables, caching the joined result in a summary table can be worth the redundancy.
  • Columnar/read-optimized stores — Azure Synapse, Redshift, BigQuery all work with wide, denormalized tables for analytical queries.

The rule: normalize first, denormalize with evidence. Never denormalize speculatively. Profile your query, confirm the join is the bottleneck, then consider denormalization. Always document why a column is denormalized and how it's kept in sync — otherwise future developers will be confused by the redundancy.

questions.tip: Denormalization is valid for read-heavy OLAP/reporting scenarios — the join overhead of fully normalized tables can kill performance at scale. Data warehouses intentionally use star/snowflake schemas which are denormalized.

questions.question: What is a deadlock in SQL and how do you prevent it?
questions.answer:
A deadlock happens when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs and both wait forever. SQL Server detects it, picks a victim (lowest cost to roll back), kills that transaction, and throws error 1205.

Classic pattern: Transaction A locks Table1 then wants Table2. Transaction B locked Table2 and wants Table1. Neither can proceed.

Prevention strategies:

  • Access tables in the same order across all transactions — breaks the circular wait.
  • Keep transactions short — acquire locks late, release early.
  • Use lower isolation levels — READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT (MVCC) eliminates most reader-writer deadlocks.
  • Index properly — full table scans lock more rows than necessary. A missing index caused a deadlock in the offline invoice project because the scan locked the entire Invoices table.
  • Optimistic concurrency — rowversion/timestamp columns; detect conflict at commit rather than locking upfront (EF Core [ConcurrencyCheck]).
followUps: How do you detect which queries are causing deadlocks?
What is a lock timeout vs deadlock?

followUpAnswers.How do you detect which queries are caus:
SQL Server records deadlock information in several places:

  • System Health Extended Events session (always running by default) — captures deadlock graphs as XML. Query it via:
-- Extract deadlock graphs from the default System Health XE session
SELECT
    xdr.value('@timestamp', 'datetime2') AS DeadlockTime,
    xdr.query('.') AS DeadlockGraph
FROM (
    SELECT CAST(target_data AS XML) AS target_data
    FROM sys.dm_xe_session_targets t
    JOIN sys.dm_xe_sessions s ON t.event_session_address = s.address
    WHERE s.name = 'system_health' AND t.target_name = 'ring_buffer'
) AS data
CROSS APPLY target_data.nodes('//RingBufferTarget/event[@name="xml_deadlock_report"]') AS XEventData(xdr)
ORDER BY DeadlockTime DESC;
  • SQL Server Error Log — deadlocks are logged if trace flag 1222 is enabled: DBCC TRACEON(1222, -1)
  • SSMS deadlock graph viewer — open the XML from Extended Events in SSMS to see a visual graph showing which processes were involved, which resources they held/wanted, and which was the victim.

The deadlock graph shows you exactly which T-SQL statements were running and which objects/rows were locked. Look for the pattern — usually a missing index causing a broader lock than needed, or two procedures accessing tables in reverse order.
followUpAnswers.What is a lock timeout vs deadlock?:
These are two different failure modes related to locking:

  • Deadlock — SQL Server detects a circular lock dependency automatically. It picks a victim and rolls back that transaction. Error 1205. You don't wait indefinitely — SQL Server resolves it within ~5 seconds. The victim's transaction is rolled back automatically.
  • Lock timeout — a transaction is waiting for a lock held by another transaction, but there's no circular dependency. If the wait exceeds LOCK_TIMEOUT, SQL Server cancels the statement (not the transaction) and throws error 1222. The transaction remains open.
-- Set lock timeout to 5 seconds (per session)
SET LOCK_TIMEOUT 5000;  -- milliseconds, -1 = wait forever (default)

-- Check current setting
SELECT @@LOCK_TIMEOUT;

Key differences:

AspectDeadlockLock Timeout
DetectionAutomatic by SQL ServerManual timeout setting
What's rolled backEntire victim transactionCurrent statement only
Error code12051222
ResolutionSQL Server resolves itApplication must handle/retry

Both require retry logic in application code. In C#, catch SqlException and check ex.Number == 1205 (deadlock) or 1222 (timeout) to implement retry with exponential backoff.

questions.tip: Deadlock graph in SQL Server Extended Events (or System Health session) shows exactly which statements deadlocked. Always check this before guessing — the actual cause is usually an index gap or a transaction that does application logic between DB calls.

questions.question: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
questions.answer:
Three very different operations that beginners often confuse:

  • DELETE — DML statement. Removes specific rows (WHERE clause optional). Logged row-by-row, can be rolled back, fires DELETE triggers, doesn't reset identity counter. Slow on large tables.
  • TRUNCATE — DDL statement. Removes ALL rows from a table. Minimally logged (logs page deallocations, not individual rows), extremely fast, resets identity counter, does NOT fire DELETE triggers. Can be rolled back if inside an explicit transaction. Cannot be used if the table has FK references pointing to it.
  • DROP — DDL statement. Removes the entire table (structure + data + indexes + constraints). Irreversible unless you have a backup.

Rule of thumb: need to wipe a staging/temp table between runs? Use TRUNCATE. Need to remove specific records? DELETE with WHERE. Never mix them up in production.
followUps: Can TRUNCATE be rolled back?
Why can't you TRUNCATE a table with foreign key references?

followUpAnswers.Can TRUNCATE be rolled back?:
Yes — if TRUNCATE is inside an explicit transaction that hasn't been committed yet. Despite being a DDL statement, TRUNCATE is fully transactional in SQL Server.

BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    TRUNCATE TABLE StagingData;  -- removes all rows
    -- Oops, wrong table!
ROLLBACK TRANSACTION;           -- rows come back!

-- Verify:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM StagingData;  -- returns original count

The confusion: People think TRUNCATE can't be rolled back because it's "minimally logged" and doesn't log individual row deletions. What it does log is the page deallocation operations — and those page deallocations are transactional. Rolling back re-allocates the pages and restores the data.

Contrast with some other databases: In MySQL/Oracle, DDL statements like TRUNCATE cause an implicit COMMIT — you can't roll them back. SQL Server is more consistent here — all DML and DDL inside an explicit transaction is rollback-safe.

Practical tip: Always wrap TRUNCATE in a transaction in scripts where you need safety, especially in ETL/staging pipelines where you want to either fully replace the data or keep the old data if something goes wrong downstream.
followUpAnswers.Why can't you TRUNCATE a table with fore:
SQL Server prevents TRUNCATEing a table when another table has a foreign key that references it, even if the referencing table is empty. The restriction exists because TRUNCATE is a minimally logged operation — it deallocates data pages without checking each row for FK violations.

-- Clients is referenced by Invoices via FK
TRUNCATE TABLE Clients;
-- Error: Cannot truncate table 'Clients' because it is being referenced by a FOREIGN KEY constraint.

Why SQL Server doesn't just check the child table: TRUNCATE bypasses row-level operations entirely. To enforce FK integrity, SQL Server would need to verify every row in the child table has no match — which is essentially a full DELETE with FK checks, defeating the performance purpose of TRUNCATE.

Workarounds:

  • Disable the FK constraint, TRUNCATE, re-enable: ALTER TABLE Invoices NOCHECK CONSTRAINT FK_Invoices_Clients; — risky, leaves integrity unchecked temporarily.
  • TRUNCATE the child table first, then the parent: always truncate in reverse FK dependency order.
  • Use DELETE instead — slower but respects FK constraints and triggers.
-- Safe order: child first, then parent
TRUNCATE TABLE Invoices;   -- child
TRUNCATE TABLE Clients;    -- parent (now no FK references)

questions.tip: TRUNCATE resets identity — if you truncate and re-insert, IDs start from 1 again. This breaks any hardcoded ID references elsewhere. Always double check.

questions.question: What are window functions in SQL? Give an example.
questions.answer:
Window functions compute values across a set of rows related to the current row — without collapsing them into a group. They're the SQL equivalent of "calculate something for each row but keep all rows visible".

Common window functions: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), LAG(), LEAD(), SUM() OVER(), AVG() OVER().

-- Rank invoices by amount within each client
SELECT
  ClientId,
  InvoiceId,
  Amount,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ClientId ORDER BY Amount DESC) AS RowNum,
  SUM(Amount) OVER (PARTITION BY ClientId) AS ClientTotal
FROM Invoices;

In the offline invoice project I used ROW_NUMBER() with PARTITION BY ClientId to find the most recent invoice per client — a classic "get latest row per group" pattern.
followUps: Difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()?
What is PARTITION BY vs GROUP BY?

followUpAnswers.Difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK:
Both assign a rank number based on ordering, but they handle ties differently:

  • RANK() — tied rows get the same rank, then the next rank skips numbers. Like competition: 1st, 1st, 3rd (no 2nd).
  • DENSE_RANK() — tied rows get the same rank, next rank does NOT skip. Like: 1st, 1st, 2nd.
  • ROW_NUMBER() — assigns a unique sequential number regardless of ties. Each tied row gets a different number (order within tie is arbitrary unless ORDER BY is fully deterministic).
SELECT
    Name,
    Salary,
    RANK()       OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rank,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS DenseRank,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS RowNum
FROM Employees;

-- Results when two employees have salary 90000:
-- Name    Salary  Rank  DenseRank  RowNum
-- Alice   100000   1       1         1
-- Bob      90000   2       2         2
-- Carol    90000   2       2         3   (same RANK/DENSE_RANK, different ROW_NUMBER)
-- Dave     80000   4       3         4   (RANK skips 3, DENSE_RANK doesn't)

When to use which: Use DENSE_RANK for "find the Nth highest distinct value" queries. Use RANK when gaps in ranking are meaningful (competition standings). Use ROW_NUMBER for pagination and deduplication where you need a unique sequential number.
followUpAnswers.What is PARTITION BY vs GROUP BY?:
Both divide rows into groups, but they produce fundamentally different output:

  • GROUP BY — collapses rows into one row per group. You lose individual row detail. Aggregates compute one value per group. The output has fewer rows than the input.
  • PARTITION BY (in window functions) — divides rows into partitions for calculation purposes, but every original row remains in the output. Aggregates compute per-partition but each row retains its own identity plus the window calculation.
-- GROUP BY: one row per ClientId (individual invoices gone)
SELECT ClientId, SUM(Amount) AS Total
FROM Invoices
GROUP BY ClientId;
-- 3 rows if 3 clients

-- PARTITION BY: every invoice row present, plus running total per client
SELECT
    InvoiceId,
    ClientId,
    Amount,
    SUM(Amount) OVER (PARTITION BY ClientId) AS ClientTotal,
    Amount / SUM(Amount) OVER (PARTITION BY ClientId) AS PctOfClientTotal
FROM Invoices;
-- Same number of rows as Invoices table

A common pattern: use PARTITION BY to show both individual values and group-level aggregates in the same result set — something GROUP BY alone can't do without a self-join or subquery.

questions.tip: ROW_NUMBER() trick for deduplication: wrap in a CTE, then DELETE WHERE RowNum > 1. This is the cleanest way to remove duplicate rows from a table.

questions.question: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression) and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A CTE is a named temporary result set defined with a WITH clause that you can reference like a table in the query that follows. It doesn't persist after the query executes — it's just a readability/organisational tool.

WITH OverdueClients AS (
  SELECT ClientId, COUNT(*) AS OverdueCount
  FROM Invoices
  WHERE DueDate < GETDATE() AND Status != 'Paid'
  GROUP BY ClientId
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
)
SELECT c.Name, oc.OverdueCount
FROM Clients c
INNER JOIN OverdueClients oc ON c.Id = oc.ClientId
ORDER BY oc.OverdueCount DESC;

When to use CTEs:

  • Break a complex query into readable steps
  • Avoid repeating subquery logic
  • Recursive queries (org chart traversal, bill of materials)
  • The "get top N per group" pattern with ROW_NUMBER()

Important: a CTE is NOT a performance optimisation. SQL Server may materialise it or inline it — the optimizer decides. If you need guaranteed materialisation, use a temp table (#Table).
followUps: What is a recursive CTE?
CTE vs temp table vs subquery — when to use which?

followUpAnswers.What is a recursive CTE?:
A recursive CTE references itself to process hierarchical or graph data. It has two parts joined by UNION ALL:

  • Anchor member — the base case, selects the starting rows (e.g., top-level managers)
  • Recursive member — references the CTE itself, adding one level per iteration
-- Traverse employee hierarchy from CEO down
WITH EmployeeHierarchy AS (
    -- Anchor: top-level employees (no manager)
    SELECT Id, Name, ManagerId, 0 AS Level
    FROM Employees
    WHERE ManagerId IS NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive: employees reporting to someone in the CTE
    SELECT e.Id, e.Name, e.ManagerId, eh.Level + 1
    FROM Employees e
    INNER JOIN EmployeeHierarchy eh ON e.ManagerId = eh.Id
)
SELECT Id, Name, Level,
       REPLICATE('  ', Level) + Name AS IndentedName
FROM EmployeeHierarchy
ORDER BY Level, Name
OPTION (MAXRECURSION 100);  -- prevent infinite loops

Other uses for recursive CTEs:

  • Bill of materials (product → sub-components → sub-sub-components)
  • Category trees (parent → child categories)
  • Finding all ancestors/descendants in a graph
  • Generating number sequences: anchor = 1, recursive = n+1

Always set OPTION (MAXRECURSION n) — default is 100, max is 32767. Set to 0 for unlimited (only when you're certain there's no cycle).
followUpAnswers.CTE vs temp table vs subquery — when to:
Each has a specific sweet spot:

  • Subquery — inline, used once, no name. Best for simple one-off calculations inside a larger query. Can't be referenced multiple times. No overhead.
  • CTE — named, defined once in the query, can be referenced multiple times in the same query (though SQL Server may re-evaluate each reference). Best for readability — break a complex query into logical steps. No guarantee of materialisation; optimizer may inline or cache at its discretion. No persistent storage overhead.
  • Temp table (#Table) — physically created in tempdb, has real statistics, can be indexed, persists for the session scope. Best for intermediate large result sets that you'll join against multiple times, or when you need reliable performance (statistics let the optimizer make better plans). Slightly higher overhead to create.
ScenarioBest Choice
Simple one-off calculationSubquery
Complex query, improve readabilityCTE
Hierarchical/recursive dataRecursive CTE
Large intermediate result, joined multiple timesTemp table
Need indexes on intermediate resultTemp table
Pass data between stored procedure callsTemp table or TVP

The key insight: CTEs are a readability tool, not a performance tool. If the CTE is referenced multiple times in a query, SQL Server may execute it multiple times (it's not automatically cached). For guaranteed single execution, use a temp table with SELECT INTO #t FROM ....

questions.tip: Recursive CTE for hierarchical data (e.g., employee → manager chain): define an anchor member + recursive member. Set OPTION (MAXRECURSION n) to avoid infinite loops.

questions.question: What is query plan and how do you use EXPLAIN/execution plan to optimise a query?
questions.answer:
The execution plan (or query plan) is the set of steps SQL Server's optimizer chooses to execute a query. It shows what operations happen in what order — table scans, index seeks, hash joins, sort operations, etc.

In SQL Server: press Ctrl+M in SSMS (actual execution plan) or Ctrl+L (estimated). Look for:

  • Table scan — reading every row. Almost always needs an index.
  • Key lookup — found the row via index but had to go back to the table for extra columns. Fix: covering index with INCLUDE.
  • Hash join — memory-intensive. Better when there's a proper index for a nested loop or merge join.
  • Sort — expensive. Can be eliminated by an ordered index.
  • Fat arrows — thick arrows between operators mean lots of rows flowing through. That's where the cost is.

In the CSInvoice project a report query had 80% cost in a sort operator. Adding an index on the ORDER BY column eliminated the sort entirely — query went from 8s to 200ms.
followUps: What is a statistics blob?
How does the optimizer choose between an index seek and scan?

followUpAnswers.What is a statistics blob?:
Statistics in SQL Server are metadata objects that contain a histogram describing the distribution of values in a column or index. The query optimizer uses statistics to estimate how many rows a filter will return — these estimates drive plan choices (which join type, whether to seek or scan, memory grants for sorts/hashes).

Each statistics object contains:

  • Header — when stats were last updated, how many rows were sampled
  • Density vector — selectivity of column combinations
  • Histogram — up to 200 "steps" showing value distribution: RANGE_HI_KEY, EQ_ROWS (rows equal to this value), RANGE_ROWS (rows between steps)
-- View statistics details
DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS ('Invoices', 'IX_Invoices_ClientId_Status');

-- Update stale statistics
UPDATE STATISTICS Invoices;
UPDATE STATISTICS Invoices IX_Invoices_ClientId_Status;  -- specific index

-- Check when stats were last updated
SELECT
    OBJECT_NAME(s.object_id) AS TableName,
    s.name AS StatsName,
    sp.last_updated,
    sp.rows,
    sp.rows_sampled
FROM sys.stats s
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_db_stats_properties(s.object_id, s.stats_id) sp
WHERE OBJECT_NAME(s.object_id) = 'Invoices';

Stale statistics are a common cause of bad execution plans. If cardinality estimates in the execution plan are wildly wrong (estimated 1 row, actual 50,000 rows), stale statistics are the likely culprit. Enable AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS (on by default) and consider AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS_ASYNC for busy systems.
followUpAnswers.How does the optimizer choose between an:
The optimizer makes this decision based on estimated selectivity — the fraction of rows it expects the filter to return:

  • Index seek — navigates the B-tree directly to matching rows. Used when the filter is highly selective (small percentage of rows match). Cost scales with the number of rows returned, not the table size.
  • Index scan — reads the entire index from start to finish (or a range). Used when a large percentage of rows match, or when no seek is possible (e.g., non-leading column of composite index, function on column).
  • Table scan (clustered index scan) — reads every page of the table. Chosen when: no useful index exists, or the optimizer estimates that >20–30% of rows will be returned (at which point a scan beats many random seeks).

The tipping point: Roughly when the expected result set exceeds ~1–5% of the table (threshold varies), SQL Server prefers a scan over many random seeks. This is correct behavior — random seeks on disk are expensive, and at high row counts a sequential scan is faster.

-- Force a specific plan hint (rarely needed, use as diagnostic)
SELECT * FROM Invoices WITH (INDEX(IX_Invoices_ClientId_Status))
WHERE ClientId = 42;

-- Check estimated rows vs actual rows in plan
-- Large discrepancy = stale statistics, need UPDATE STATISTICS

If the optimizer is choosing a scan when you expect a seek, check: (1) is the leading column of the index in the WHERE clause? (2) Is there a function wrapping the column (e.g., YEAR(CreatedAt))? (3) Are statistics up to date?

questions.tip: Statistics are the query optimizer's eyes — if stats are stale, it guesses row counts wrong and picks a bad plan. Run UPDATE STATISTICS TableName or enable AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS.

questions.question: What is the N+1 problem in database queries?
questions.answer:
The N+1 problem is when you execute 1 query to get N parent records, then fire 1 query per parent to get its children — totalling N+1 queries instead of 1.

// BAD — N+1 with EF Core
var orders = await _db.Orders.ToListAsync();          // 1 query
foreach (var o in orders)
{
    var items = await _db.OrderItems                   // N queries!
        .Where(i => i.OrderId == o.Id)
        .ToListAsync();
}

// GOOD — single query with Include
var orders = await _db.Orders
    .Include(o => o.OrderItems)
    .ToListAsync();  // 1 query with JOIN

In FolioTrack I caught this when profiling with EF Core logging — loading 50 portfolios was firing 51 queries. Added .Include(p => p.Holdings) and it became 1 query. EF Core's AsSplitQuery() is another option for very wide result sets.
followUps: What is AsSplitQuery() in EF Core?
How do you detect N+1 in production?

followUpAnswers.What is AsSplitQuery() in EF Core?:
AsSplitQuery() is an EF Core feature that splits a single query with multiple collection includes into separate SQL queries, one per collection, instead of one large JOIN.

The problem it solves: When you have multiple collection-level includes (e.g., Order → OrderItems AND Order → OrderPayments), EF Core generates a single JOIN query. With large collections, this produces a Cartesian explosion — if an order has 10 items and 5 payments, each row appears 50 times (10×5) in the result, multiplying network and memory usage.

// Single query — Cartesian explosion if both collections are large
var orders = await _db.Orders
    .Include(o => o.OrderItems)
    .Include(o => o.Payments)
    .ToListAsync();

// Split query — 3 separate SQL queries, no Cartesian product
var orders = await _db.Orders
    .Include(o => o.OrderItems)
    .Include(o => o.Payments)
    .AsSplitQuery()
    .ToListAsync();

Trade-offs:

  • Split queries eliminate Cartesian explosions on multiple collection includes
  • But they're multiple round trips — if the network is slow, multiple queries can be slower than one large one
  • Not atomic — data could change between the separate queries (not wrapped in a transaction by default)

Use AsSplitQuery when you have 2+ collection includes and the result set is noticeably large. Configure as default: optionsBuilder.UseQuerySplittingBehavior(QuerySplittingBehavior.SplitQuery).
followUpAnswers.How do you detect N+1 in production?:
Several approaches, from development to production:

  • EF Core logging (development) — log all SQL to console or debug output. Look for the same query repeating in a loop:
    optionsBuilder.LogTo(Console.WriteLine, LogLevel.Information);
  • MiniProfiler — NuGet package that adds an on-page profiler showing all SQL queries, execution time, and duplicate detection. Ideal for staging/UAT environments.
  • EF Core's EnableDetailedErrors + EnableSensitiveDataLogging — shows parameter values so you can see the same query running with 50 different IDs.
  • Application Insights / OpenTelemetry — in production, distributed tracing captures SQL spans. If a single HTTP request generates 50+ SQL calls, it's visible in the trace timeline.
  • SQL Server side — sys.dm_exec_query_stats + sys.dm_exec_sql_text to find the most frequently executed queries. A query running thousands of times per minute on a small table is a red flag.
-- Top queries by execution count in plan cache
SELECT TOP 20
    qs.execution_count,
    qs.total_elapsed_time / qs.execution_count AS avg_ms,
    SUBSTRING(st.text, 1, 200) AS query_snippet
FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(qs.sql_handle) st
ORDER BY qs.execution_count DESC;

questions.tip: Enable EF Core SQL logging in development: optionsBuilder.LogTo(Console.WriteLine, LogLevel.Information). If you see the same query firing in a loop, you have N+1. MiniProfiler is excellent for this in staging.

questions.question: What is the difference between clustered and non-clustered indexes?
questions.answer:
Clustered index — the table's data is physically sorted and stored in this index's order. Only ONE per table. SQL Server creates it on the primary key by default. Think of it as the table itself organised as a B-tree.

Non-clustered index — a separate structure that stores the index key columns + a pointer (row locator) back to the actual data row. You can have up to 999 per table in SQL Server. Think of it as a book index at the back.

Key implications:

  • Clustered index seeks are faster for range scans because data is physically ordered.
  • Non-clustered index requires an extra hop (key lookup) to get columns not in the index — unless it's a covering index.
  • Inserting rows in random order into a clustered index causes page splits — that's why GUIDs as PKs are problematic. Use sequential IDs (NEWSEQUENTIALID()) or integer identity.

In the CSInvoice DB, switching from GUID PKs to INT IDENTITY for the Invoices table reduced index fragmentation from 70% to under 5% and improved insert throughput by 40%.
followUps: What is index fragmentation?
Why are GUIDs bad as clustered index keys?
What is FILL FACTOR?

followUpAnswers.What is index fragmentation?:
Index fragmentation occurs when the logical order of index pages no longer matches their physical order on disk (external fragmentation), or when index pages have a lot of empty space (internal fragmentation). Both reduce I/O efficiency.

Fragmentation builds up naturally over time from INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations — especially random INSERTs that split B-tree pages. A clustered index on a GUID column fragments very quickly because GUIDs are random; every insert is potentially a mid-tree insertion causing a page split.

-- Check fragmentation percentage
SELECT
    OBJECT_NAME(ips.object_id) AS TableName,
    i.name AS IndexName,
    ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent,
    ips.page_count
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(DB_ID(), NULL, NULL, NULL, 'SAMPLED') ips
JOIN sys.indexes i
    ON ips.object_id = i.object_id AND ips.index_id = i.index_id
WHERE ips.page_count > 1000  -- ignore tiny indexes
ORDER BY ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent DESC;

-- Fix: reorganize (online, light defrag)
ALTER INDEX IX_Invoices_ClientId ON Invoices REORGANIZE;

-- Fix: rebuild (more thorough, briefly locks in non-enterprise)
ALTER INDEX IX_Invoices_ClientId ON Invoices REBUILD;

Maintenance schedule: check monthly, reorganize indexes with 10–30% fragmentation, rebuild those over 30%.
followUpAnswers.Why are GUIDs bad as clustered index key:
GUIDs (UUIDs) as clustered index keys cause severe fragmentation because they're randomly generated — each new GUID value inserts into a random position in the B-tree, splitting pages constantly.

What happens on a page split:

  • SQL Server must split the full page into two half-full pages
  • New page must be allocated somewhere else on disk (not adjacent)
  • Every page split = extra I/O write + log entry
  • Result: index becomes highly fragmented very quickly, pages are 50% full instead of 80%+

Concrete impact: In CSInvoice with GUID PKs, after 6 months of production use, the clustered index was 70%+ fragmented — reads required 2× the I/O of a compact index. Insert speed degraded ~40% from page split overhead.

Solutions:

  • INT IDENTITY — sequential, always appends to the end of the B-tree. Near-zero fragmentation. Simple and fast. Limitation: not globally unique across databases/systems.
  • NEWSEQUENTIALID() — SQL Server function generating sequential GUIDs. Still a GUID (globally unique) but ordered, avoiding random splits. Use this if you need GUID-shaped IDs.
  • ULID / UUID v7 — application-generated time-ordered IDs. Increasingly popular in modern systems.
followUpAnswers.What is FILL FACTOR?:
FILL FACTOR specifies how full SQL Server should pack each leaf-level page when creating or rebuilding an index — expressed as a percentage (1–100). The remaining space is reserved for future insertions.

-- Create index with 80% fill factor (leaves 20% free space)
CREATE INDEX IX_Invoices_ClientId ON Invoices (ClientId)
WITH (FILLFACTOR = 80);

-- Apply during rebuild
ALTER INDEX IX_Invoices_ClientId ON Invoices
REBUILD WITH (FILLFACTOR = 80);

-- Default server-wide fill factor
EXEC sp_configure 'fill factor', 80;
RECONFIGURE;

How to choose fill factor:

  • 100 (default) — pages are completely full. Maximum read density, minimum storage. Best for read-only or append-only tables (no mid-tree inserts).
  • 80–90 — reasonable default for OLTP tables with regular inserts/updates. Leaves headroom to absorb some growth before page splits.
  • 50–70 — for heavily fragmented indexes on tables with frequent random inserts. Accept lower page density to reduce page split frequency.

Key insight: Fill factor only applies at the time of index creation/rebuild. As data is inserted afterward, pages fill up and splits eventually occur regardless. Fill factor buys time between maintenance windows — it's not a permanent fix for fragmentation, just a knob to tune how quickly fragmentation returns.

questions.tip: GUID clustered index problem: random GUIDs insert into the middle of the B-tree causing constant page splits and high fragmentation. Use NEWSEQUENTIALID() or a sequential integer for clustered index columns.

questions.question: What is a view in SQL and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A view is a saved SELECT query that acts like a virtual table. The view definition is stored in the database; when you query the view, SQL Server executes the underlying SELECT transparently.

Use cases:

  • Simplify complex joins — expose a joined result as a simple table for reporting queries.
  • Security — grant users access to the view without exposing underlying tables (mask sensitive columns like salary, SSN).
  • Backward compatibility — when you rename/restructure tables, keep the old view name pointing to the new structure so existing queries don't break.

Indexed (materialised) view — SQL Server can create a clustered index on a view, making it physically stored and auto-maintained. Huge performance win for aggregate reporting queries.

In the offline invoice project I created a vw_ClientInvoiceSummary view joining Clients, Invoices, and Payments — the reporting module queried this view instead of writing the same 4-table join everywhere.
followUps: What is an indexed/materialised view?
Can you UPDATE through a view?
View vs stored procedure — when to use which?

followUpAnswers.What is an indexed/materialised view?:
An indexed view (called a materialized view in other databases) is a view with a unique clustered index created on it. This causes SQL Server to physically store the view's result set on disk and automatically maintain it as the underlying tables change.

Requirements for indexed views in SQL Server:

  • Must use WITH SCHEMABINDING
  • All table references must use two-part names (schema.table)
  • No outer joins, subqueries, CTEs, or non-deterministic functions
  • Aggregates: only COUNT_BIG(*), SUM allowed (not AVG — computed from SUM/COUNT_BIG)
CREATE VIEW dbo.vw_ClientInvoiceTotals
WITH SCHEMABINDING
AS
SELECT
    c.Id AS ClientId,
    c.Name,
    COUNT_BIG(*) AS InvoiceCount,
    SUM(i.Amount) AS TotalAmount
FROM dbo.Clients c
JOIN dbo.Invoices i ON c.Id = i.ClientId
GROUP BY c.Id, c.Name;
GO

-- Create the index to materialise it
CREATE UNIQUE CLUSTERED INDEX IX_vw_ClientInvoiceTotals
ON dbo.vw_ClientInvoiceTotals (ClientId);

Performance impact: Queries against the base tables can automatically use the indexed view without explicitly referencing it (optimizer matches). Major win for aggregate-heavy reporting queries. Trade-off: every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on base tables must also update the view's stored data — adds write overhead.
followUpAnswers.Can you UPDATE through a view?:
Yes, but only under specific conditions. SQL Server allows DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) through a view when the view is updatable:

A view is updatable if:

  • It references only one base table (no multi-table joins)
  • It doesn't use DISTINCT, GROUP BY, HAVING, or aggregate functions
  • It doesn't use UNION, INTERSECT, or EXCEPT
  • The columns being modified map directly to base table columns (no computed columns)
-- Simple view: updatable
CREATE VIEW vw_ActiveClients AS
SELECT Id, Name, Email FROM Clients WHERE IsActive = 1;

-- This UPDATE works (single table, direct column mapping)
UPDATE vw_ActiveClients SET Email = 'new@email.com' WHERE Id = 42;

-- A join view is NOT directly updatable
CREATE VIEW vw_InvoiceClient AS
SELECT i.Id, i.Amount, c.Name
FROM Invoices i JOIN Clients c ON i.ClientId = c.Id;

-- INSTEAD OF trigger makes join views updatable
CREATE TRIGGER trg_vw_InvoiceClient_Update
ON vw_InvoiceClient INSTEAD OF UPDATE
AS BEGIN
    UPDATE Invoices SET Amount = i.Amount FROM inserted i WHERE Invoices.Id = i.Id;
END;
followUpAnswers.View vs stored procedure — when to use w:
The key distinction is what the consumer needs to do with it:

CriterionUse a ViewUse a Stored Procedure
Usage in queryNeed to use it inside a SELECT, JOIN, WHERECalled standalone, not composable in queries
ParametersNo parameters needed (or use inline TVF instead)Need input parameters to filter/control behaviour
Data modificationRead-only (mostly)INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations needed
CompositionCan JOIN views together, use in CTEsCannot be JOINed directly
SecurityColumn-level security, hide table structureRow-level logic, complex business rules

Practical rules:

  • If you need a parameterized view, use an inline table-valued function (iTVF) instead — it's inlined by the optimizer like a view but accepts parameters.
  • Use a view for reusable read queries that other queries will compose with.
  • Use a stored procedure for business operations: create order, process payment, run nightly batch.
  • In EF Core applications, views map cleanly as keyless entity types; stored procedures are called via FromSqlRaw or ExecuteSqlRaw.

questions.tip: Views are NOT performance optimisations by default — SQL Server inlines the view definition into your query. The optimizer sees the same SQL whether you use a view or write the query directly. Only indexed views materialise data.

questions.question: How does SQL Server handle concurrency? Explain optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency.
questions.answer:
Pessimistic concurrency — lock data when you read it, prevent others from modifying it until you're done. SQL Server's default lock-based approach. Safe but causes blocking under heavy concurrent load.

Optimistic concurrency — don't lock at read time. At commit time, check if data changed since you read it. If changed, reject the update (conflict). Works well when conflicts are rare.

In SQL Server: Optimistic concurrency is implemented via rowversion (or timestamp) columns. When you read a row you capture its rowversion. At update time you include WHERE RowVersion = @capturedVersion — if it changed, 0 rows affected = conflict detected.

In EF Core: Use [Timestamp] attribute or .IsRowVersion() in fluent API. EF Core automatically throws DbUpdateConcurrencyException when 0 rows affected.

In WalletVerification we used optimistic concurrency on the Wallet entity to prevent double top-up from rapid button clicks. The second click would always fail the rowversion check and the user got a friendly retry message.
followUps: What is DbUpdateConcurrencyException and how do you handle it?
When to use pessimistic vs optimistic?

followUpAnswers.What is DbUpdateConcurrencyException and:
DbUpdateConcurrencyException is thrown by EF Core's SaveChangesAsync() when the number of rows affected by an UPDATE or DELETE is 0 — meaning the row was modified or deleted by another process after you read it. It's the EF Core mechanism for optimistic concurrency conflict detection.

// Entity with concurrency token
public class Wallet
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public decimal Balance { get; set; }
    [Timestamp]  // EF Core concurrency token
    public byte[] RowVersion { get; set; }
}

// Handling the exception
try
{
    var wallet = await _db.Wallets.FindAsync(walletId);
    wallet.Balance += amount;
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();  // throws if row changed since FindAsync
}
catch (DbUpdateConcurrencyException ex)
{
    var entry = ex.Entries.Single();
    var dbValues = await entry.GetDatabaseValuesAsync();  // current DB values

    if (dbValues == null)
        throw new Exception("Wallet was deleted by another process");

    // Option 1: Retry with fresh values (client wins)
    entry.OriginalValues.SetValues(dbValues);
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();

    // Option 2: Reject the update (database wins)
    entry.CurrentValues.SetValues(dbValues);
    throw new ConcurrencyConflictException("Wallet modified concurrently, please retry");
}

Resolution strategies:

  • Client wins — overwrite the database with the client's change regardless.
  • Database wins — discard the client's change, refresh with current DB values.
  • Merge — inspect which properties changed and apply a business-level merge (complex, use sparingly).
followUpAnswers.When to use pessimistic vs optimistic?:
The choice depends on your conflict frequency and cost of conflicts:

  • Use optimistic concurrency when:
    • Conflicts are rare — most users work on different data
    • The cost of retrying on conflict is low
    • High read throughput is needed — optimistic avoids all read locks, no blocking
    • Examples: user profile edits, content management, most CRUD screens
  • Use pessimistic concurrency when:
    • Conflicts are frequent or the cost of conflict is high
    • You cannot afford to lose an update (financial operations)
    • Two users regularly update the same small set of rows
    • Examples: inventory decrement (prevent overselling), ticket booking (prevent double-booking), wallet debit
// Pessimistic: acquire UPDLOCK before reading
await _db.Database.ExecuteSqlRawAsync(
    "SELECT * FROM Inventory WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK) WHERE ProductId = @id",
    new SqlParameter("@id", productId));
// Now hold the lock while checking and decrementing quantity
// Other transactions wanting to update this row will wait

My rule: Start with optimistic concurrency (simpler code, better scalability). Switch to pessimistic only for specific, identified high-conflict operations where retrying is unacceptable.

questions.tip: Pessimistic concurrency in EF Core: use dbContext.Database.ExecuteSqlRawAsync("SELECT ... WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK)") to hold a write lock. Useful for inventory decrement operations where you absolutely cannot allow two threads to read the same value simultaneously.

questions.question: What is a foreign key and how does it enforce referential integrity?
questions.answer:
A foreign key is a column (or set of columns) in one table that references the primary key of another table. SQL Server enforces that the referenced row actually exists — this is called referential integrity.

What SQL Server prevents:

  • Inserting a row in the child table referencing a non-existent parent.
  • Deleting a parent row that has child rows (unless you define ON DELETE CASCADE or ON DELETE SET NULL).
  • Updating the parent PK to a value that orphans children.
ALTER TABLE Invoices
ADD CONSTRAINT FK_Invoices_Clients
FOREIGN KEY (ClientId) REFERENCES Clients(Id)
ON DELETE RESTRICT  -- block delete if invoices exist
ON UPDATE CASCADE;  -- propagate PK changes

Performance note: Foreign keys implicitly create an index requirement on the referencing column (child side). SQL Server doesn't auto-create this index — you should add one manually to prevent full scans during cascade operations.
followUps: What is ON DELETE CASCADE vs ON DELETE SET NULL?
Can you temporarily disable FK constraints?

followUpAnswers.What is ON DELETE CASCADE vs ON DELETE S:
These are referential actions that define what happens to child rows when a parent row is deleted (or updated):

  • ON DELETE CASCADE — automatically deletes all child rows when the parent is deleted. Useful when child rows have no meaning without the parent (e.g., order line items when the order is deleted).
  • ON DELETE SET NULL — sets the FK column to NULL in all child rows when the parent is deleted. Useful when the child can exist without a parent (e.g., a post's author is deleted, but the post itself remains with a NULL AuthorId).
  • ON DELETE SET DEFAULT — sets the FK column to its default value on parent delete.
  • ON DELETE RESTRICT / NO ACTION (default) — prevent deletion of parent row if child rows exist. The delete fails with a FK violation error.
-- Cascade delete: delete client removes all their invoices
ALTER TABLE Invoices ADD CONSTRAINT FK_Invoices_Clients
FOREIGN KEY (ClientId) REFERENCES Clients(Id)
ON DELETE CASCADE;

-- Set null: delete department nulls the employee's DepartmentId
ALTER TABLE Employees ADD CONSTRAINT FK_Employees_Departments
FOREIGN KEY (DepartmentId) REFERENCES Departments(Id)
ON DELETE SET NULL;

Caution with CASCADE: Cascades can chain — deleting one row can trigger cascades through multiple tables. Always trace the cascade chain before enabling. SQL Server prevents circular cascades and will error if it detects a cycle.
followUpAnswers.Can you temporarily disable FK constrain:
Yes. SQL Server allows disabling FK constraints for bulk load operations, data migrations, or ETL processes where you need to insert data in an order that temporarily violates referential integrity.

-- Disable a specific FK constraint
ALTER TABLE Invoices NOCHECK CONSTRAINT FK_Invoices_Clients;

-- Disable ALL constraints on a table
ALTER TABLE Invoices NOCHECK CONSTRAINT ALL;

-- Re-enable and verify existing data
ALTER TABLE Invoices WITH CHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT FK_Invoices_Clients;
-- WITH CHECK = validates existing rows, not just new ones
-- WITHOUT CHECK = re-enables but marks constraint as "not trusted" (WITH NOCHECK)

-- Check if constraints are trusted
SELECT name, is_disabled, is_not_trusted
FROM sys.foreign_keys
WHERE parent_object_id = OBJECT_ID('Invoices');

Risks and warnings:

  • Disabling FK constraints allows orphaned rows to be inserted — you can end up with data in an inconsistent state.
  • If you re-enable with NOCHECK (not re-validating), the constraint is marked is_not_trusted = 1. SQL Server won't use the FK constraint for query optimization (foreign key elimination) when it's not trusted.
  • Always re-enable with WITH CHECK after bulk loads to re-validate data integrity and restore the trusted status.
  • Prefer disabling in a transaction so any integrity issues can be rolled back.

questions.tip: Always index the FK column in the child table. Without it, any delete from the parent causes a full table scan of the child to check for references. SQL Server warns you in the execution plan but won't create the index for you.

questions.question: What is database sharding and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
Sharding is horizontal partitioning — splitting a large database table across multiple physical databases (shards) based on a shard key. Each shard holds a subset of the rows.

Example: 100M user records split across 10 shards by UserId % 10. User ID 42 goes to shard 2, user ID 103 goes to shard 3.

When to consider sharding:

  • Single server can no longer handle the write throughput
  • Table is too large for indexes to fit in memory
    • Typically when you're past 1-5TB on a single server

Problems sharding introduces:

  • Cross-shard joins become application-level joins (expensive)
  • Transactions across shards require distributed transaction protocols
  • Rebalancing when you add a shard is painful
  • Queries not filtered by shard key must hit ALL shards (scatter-gather)

I haven't implemented sharding in my projects — at scale for FolioTrack and the invoice systems we were well within single-server limits. I'd exhaust vertical scaling and read replicas before sharding.
followUps: What is consistent hashing and why is it used for sharding?
How does Azure SQL Elastic Pool relate to sharding?

followUpAnswers.What is consistent hashing and why is it:
Consistent hashing is an algorithm for distributing data across a variable number of nodes (shards) in a way that minimises data movement when nodes are added or removed.

The problem with simple modulo sharding: If you shard by userId % N and add a shard (N becomes N+1), almost every row needs to move to a new shard because every modulo result changes. This is catastrophically expensive for re-sharding.

How consistent hashing solves it:

  • Imagine a circle (hash ring) with positions 0 to 2^32.
  • Each shard node is hashed to a position on the ring.
  • Each key is hashed to a position on the ring; it belongs to the first shard node clockwise from it.
  • When you add a shard, only the keys between the new node and its predecessor need to move — roughly 1/N of all data.
  • When you remove a shard, only its keys move to the next node.

Virtual nodes: Real consistent hashing implementations use virtual nodes (multiple hash positions per physical node) to distribute load more evenly and handle node heterogeneity.

Used by: Apache Cassandra, Amazon DynamoDB, Redis Cluster all use consistent hashing or variants for their distributed architectures. If you're designing a sharded system from scratch, consistent hashing is the standard approach.
followUpAnswers.How does Azure SQL Elastic Pool relate t:
Azure SQL Elastic Pool is a Microsoft Azure resource-sharing model for multiple Azure SQL databases — it's not the same as application-level sharding, but it enables a multi-database architecture efficiently.

Elastic Pool:

  • Multiple Azure SQL databases share a pool of DTUs or vCores (compute/storage resources).
  • Databases within the pool can burst above their individual allocation using pooled resources.
  • Cost-effective for SaaS multi-tenant applications where each tenant has their own database but databases have variable/off-peak usage.

Elastic Database Tools for sharding: Azure provides the Elastic Database Client Library (EDCL) which adds application-level sharding capabilities on top of Elastic Pools:

  • Shard map manager — tracks which data range lives on which database (shard).
  • Data-dependent routing — application-level routing that connects to the correct shard database based on a shard key.
  • Multi-shard queries — fan-out queries across all shards and merge results.

Practical view: For a SaaS app where each customer gets their own isolated database, Elastic Pool + EDCL is Azure's managed sharding story. It's far more manageable than hand-rolling cross-server sharding.

questions.tip: Sharding is often premature optimisation. Most apps never need it. First try: better indexes, query optimization, read replicas, caching, table partitioning (SQL Server built-in). Sharding adds enormous operational complexity.

questions.question: Write a SQL query to find the second highest salary from an Employee table.
questions.answer:
Classic interview question with multiple valid approaches:

-- Method 1: Using subquery (works everywhere)
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighest
FROM Employee
WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);

-- Method 2: Using OFFSET-FETCH (SQL Server 2012+, cleanest)
SELECT DISTINCT Salary
FROM Employee
ORDER BY Salary DESC
OFFSET 1 ROWS FETCH NEXT 1 ROWS ONLY;

-- Method 3: Using DENSE_RANK() (handles ties correctly)
SELECT Salary
FROM (
  SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk
  FROM Employee
) ranked
WHERE Rnk = 2;

Why DENSE_RANK is best: If two employees share the highest salary, MAX(salary) WHERE salary < MAX correctly finds the second distinct value. But DENSE_RANK handles the generalised "Nth highest" case cleanly — just change WHERE Rnk = 2 to WHERE Rnk = N.
followUps: Write it for Nth highest salary
What if there are duplicate salaries?

followUpAnswers.Write it for Nth highest salary:
The cleanest approach uses DENSE_RANK() — simply parameterize the rank value:

-- Nth highest DISTINCT salary using DENSE_RANK
DECLARE @N INT = 3;  -- change to any N

SELECT Salary
FROM (
    SELECT
        Salary,
        DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk
    FROM Employee
) ranked
WHERE Rnk = @N;

-- Alternative: OFFSET-FETCH with DISTINCT
SELECT DISTINCT Salary
FROM Employee
ORDER BY Salary DESC
OFFSET (@N - 1) ROWS
FETCH NEXT 1 ROWS ONLY;

-- Stored procedure version for interviews
CREATE PROCEDURE GetNthHighestSalary @N INT
AS
BEGIN
    SELECT Salary
    FROM (
        SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk
        FROM Employee
    ) r
    WHERE Rnk = @N;
END;

Edge cases to mention:
