  • What if N is greater than the number of distinct salaries? → Return NULL or empty result set.
  • What if the table is empty? → Return NULL.
  • The OFFSET-FETCH version requires SQL Server 2012+.
  • DENSE_RANK is usually preferred for interview answers because it handles duplicates correctly and is explicit about what "Nth highest" means.
followUpAnswers.What if there are duplicate salaries?:
The answer depends on what the interviewer means by "second highest" — this is worth clarifying:

  • Second highest distinct salary value — if three people earn 90,000 (the max) and two earn 80,000, the second highest distinct value is 80,000. DENSE_RANK() with WHERE Rnk = 2 gives this result correctly.
  • Second highest row by salary — might mean the employee with the second-highest position when sorted by salary. ROW_NUMBER() handles this, but the result could still be 90,000 if the second row also has the maximum salary.
-- Sample data illustrating the difference:
-- Emp  Salary  ROW_NUMBER  RANK  DENSE_RANK
-- A    100000     1          1       1
-- B     90000     2          2       2
-- C     90000     3          2       2     ← same DENSE_RANK as B
-- D     80000     4          4       3     ← RANK skips 3, DENSE_RANK continues

-- "Second highest salary value" = 90000 (use DENSE_RANK)
SELECT Salary FROM (
    SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS dr
    FROM Employee
) r WHERE dr = 2;
-- Returns: 90000

-- "All employees with the second-highest salary"
-- (multiple rows if there are ties)
SELECT Name, Salary FROM (
    SELECT Name, Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS dr
    FROM Employee
) r WHERE dr = 2;
-- Returns both B and C

Always state your assumption: "I'll interpret this as the second highest distinct salary value, using DENSE_RANK." This demonstrates you understand the subtlety.

questions.tip: Always clarify: second highest distinct value or second highest row? If two rows have salary 100000 (the max), is 100000 still the "second highest"? DENSE_RANK gives you distinct-value ranking; ROW_NUMBER gives you row ranking.

questions.question: What are SQL triggers and when should you use them?
questions.answer:
A trigger is a special stored procedure that automatically executes in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events on a table (DML triggers) or DDL events (schema changes).

DML trigger types:

  • AFTER trigger — fires after the DML completes. Can still rollback. Most common use: audit logging.
  • INSTEAD OF trigger — fires instead of the DML. Common use: making non-updatable views updatable.
-- Audit trigger: log every invoice status change
CREATE TRIGGER trg_Invoice_StatusChange
ON Invoices AFTER UPDATE
AS
BEGIN
  IF UPDATE(Status)
    INSERT INTO InvoiceAudit (InvoiceId, OldStatus, NewStatus, ChangedAt)
    SELECT d.Id, d.Status, i.Status, GETUTCDATE()
    FROM inserted i
    INNER JOIN deleted d ON i.Id = d.Id;
END;

When NOT to use triggers: Application logic in triggers is invisible to developers, hard to debug, can't be unit tested, fire for every row (performance at scale). I prefer application-level audit interceptors in EF Core over DB triggers for most cases.
followUps: What are the inserted and deleted pseudo-tables in triggers?
Can a trigger call another trigger (nested triggers)?

followUpAnswers.What are the inserted and deleted pseudo:
inserted and deleted are virtual tables automatically available inside a trigger body. They contain the rows affected by the DML operation:

  • INSERT trigger — inserted contains the new rows being added. deleted is empty.
  • DELETE trigger — deleted contains the rows being removed. inserted is empty.
  • UPDATE trigger — deleted contains the old (before) row values. inserted contains the new (after) row values. Think of UPDATE as DELETE + INSERT.
-- Using both tables in an UPDATE trigger
CREATE TRIGGER trg_InvoiceAmountChange
ON Invoices AFTER UPDATE
AS
BEGIN
    -- Only fire if Amount changed
    IF UPDATE(Amount)
    BEGIN
        INSERT INTO InvoiceAuditLog
            (InvoiceId, FieldChanged, OldValue, NewValue, ChangedAt)
        SELECT
            i.Id,
            'Amount',
            CAST(d.Amount AS NVARCHAR(50)),  -- from deleted (old)
            CAST(i.Amount AS NVARCHAR(50)),  -- from inserted (new)
            GETUTCDATE()
        FROM inserted i
        JOIN deleted d ON i.Id = d.Id
        WHERE i.Amount != d.Amount;  -- only rows where Amount actually changed
    END
END;

Important: Triggers fire once per DML statement, not per row. If an UPDATE affects 1,000 rows, the trigger fires once and inserted/deleted each contain 1,000 rows. Never write trigger logic assuming they're single-row — always join on the PK.
followUpAnswers.Can a trigger call another trigger (nest:
Yes — SQL Server supports nested triggers, where a DML operation inside a trigger fires another trigger on a different (or the same) table. Nesting is enabled by default.

-- Check if nested triggers are enabled
SELECT name, value_in_use
FROM sys.configurations
WHERE name = 'nested triggers';

-- Disable nested triggers server-wide
EXEC sp_configure 'nested triggers', 0;
RECONFIGURE;

Nesting depth: SQL Server allows up to 32 levels of trigger nesting. If this limit is exceeded, the transaction is rolled back.

Recursive triggers: A trigger that fires an event on its own table. Controlled by the database-level RECURSIVE_TRIGGERS option (off by default). Recursive triggers fire when a trigger on Table A performs a DML on Table A, which fires the same trigger again.

-- Check recursive trigger setting per database
SELECT name, is_recursive_triggers_on FROM sys.databases WHERE name = DB_NAME();

-- Enable recursive triggers
ALTER DATABASE MyDatabase SET RECURSIVE_TRIGGERS ON;

Recommendation: Keep trigger logic simple and avoid triggering cascades. Nested trigger chains are extremely hard to debug and reason about. If you find yourself writing triggers that fire triggers, it's a signal the design needs rethinking — move that logic to the application layer.

questions.tip: EF Core alternative to audit triggers: Override SaveChangesAsync() in DbContext and inspect ChangeTracker.Entries() — same audit capability but fully testable, debuggable, and under version control.

questions.question: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
questions.answer:
Both combine result sets from multiple SELECT statements, but they handle duplicates differently:

  • UNION — removes duplicate rows from the combined result. Requires an implicit DISTINCT operation which has a sorting/hashing cost.
  • UNION ALL — keeps ALL rows including duplicates. No deduplication step — faster.
-- UNION: removes duplicates (shared customers appear once)
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersArchive
UNION
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersCurrent;

-- UNION ALL: keeps duplicates (faster when duplicates OK)
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersArchive
UNION ALL
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersCurrent;

Rule: use UNION ALL by default unless you specifically need deduplication. The extra DISTINCT pass in UNION can be very expensive on large result sets.
followUps: What requirements must both SELECT statements meet for UNION?
Can you ORDER BY in a UNION query?

followUpAnswers.What requirements must both SELECT state:
For UNION (or UNION ALL) to work, both SELECT statements must satisfy these rules:

  • Same number of columns — both SELECT lists must have the same column count.
  • Compatible data types — corresponding columns must have compatible types. SQL Server will attempt implicit conversion (e.g., INT and BIGINT are compatible), but incompatible types (e.g., INT and NVARCHAR with no conversion) will error.
  • Column names come from the first SELECT — the output column names are taken from the first query's column aliases or column names. The second query's column names are ignored.
-- Works: same count, compatible types, names from first SELECT
SELECT Id AS UserId, Name, NULL AS Email  -- 3 columns
FROM Customers
UNION ALL
SELECT EmployeeId, FullName, WorkEmail     -- 3 columns, compatible types
FROM Employees;

-- Fails: different column counts
SELECT Id, Name FROM Customers
UNION
SELECT Id, Name, Email FROM Employees;  -- ERROR: different number of columns

-- Tip: use NULL or cast to align column lists
SELECT 'Customer' AS Source, Id, Name, Email
FROM Customers
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Employee', EmployeeId, FullName, NULL  -- NULL as placeholder
FROM Employees;

A common trick: add a literal source column ('Customer', 'Employee') to indicate which table each row came from after the UNION.
followUpAnswers.Can you ORDER BY in a UNION query?:
Yes, but only once at the very end of the entire UNION. You cannot place ORDER BY inside individual SELECT statements that are part of a UNION.

-- CORRECT: single ORDER BY at the end applies to the entire combined result
SELECT Id, Name, 'Customer' AS Source FROM Customers
UNION ALL
SELECT Id, Name, 'Employee' FROM Employees
ORDER BY Name ASC;  -- sorts the combined result

-- INCORRECT: cannot ORDER BY inside individual SELECT parts
SELECT Id, Name FROM Customers ORDER BY Name  -- ERROR
UNION ALL
SELECT Id, Name FROM Employees;

-- ORDER BY can reference column name from first SELECT or column position
SELECT Id, Name FROM Customers
UNION ALL
SELECT EmployeeId, FullName FROM Employees
ORDER BY 2 DESC;  -- order by 2nd column (Name/FullName) by position

Workaround if you need per-source ordering: Add an ordering column to each SELECT and order by that, then the data column:

SELECT 1 AS SortGroup, Id, Name FROM Customers
UNION ALL
SELECT 2 AS SortGroup, Id, Name FROM Employees
ORDER BY SortGroup, Name;

questions.tip: Column count and data types must match in both SELECT statements. The column names come from the first SELECT. You can ORDER BY only at the end of the entire UNION, not per-SELECT.

questions.question: How do you handle pagination in SQL?
questions.answer:
Two common approaches, each with trade-offs:

OFFSET-FETCH (SQL Server 2012+):

SELECT Id, Title, CreatedAt
FROM Articles
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC
OFFSET (@Page - 1) * @PageSize ROWS
FETCH NEXT @PageSize ROWS ONLY;

Keyset pagination (cursor-based, better for large tables):

-- First page: no filter
SELECT TOP (@PageSize) Id, CreatedAt FROM Articles ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC;

-- Next pages: use last seen value as cursor
SELECT TOP (@PageSize) Id, CreatedAt
FROM Articles
WHERE CreatedAt < @LastSeenCreatedAt
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC;

Why keyset is better at scale: OFFSET scans and discards all preceding rows — page 1000 with 20-row pages means scanning 19,980 rows to throw them away. Keyset jumps directly to the cursor position using an index seek.

In FolioTrack's news feed I used keyset pagination on the Angular client with a ?cursor= query param — no visible degradation at any page depth.
followUps: What are the limitations of keyset pagination?
How do you get total count efficiently with pagination?

followUpAnswers.What are the limitations of keyset pagin:
Keyset pagination is faster at scale but has real limitations:

  • No random page access — you can only go forward (or backward with a reversed cursor). You can't jump to "page 47" directly. Suitable for infinite scroll / "load more" UIs, not traditional numbered page navigation.
  • Cursor column must be unique and indexed — if you paginate by CreatedAt and two rows have the exact same timestamp, you may skip or duplicate rows. Fix: use a tie-breaking secondary sort column (e.g., ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC, Id DESC) and include both values in the cursor.
  • Sort column must be stable — if the sort column can change (e.g., a "last updated" timestamp), rows may shift between pages as the user navigates, causing misses or duplicates.
  • Compound cursors are complex — when you sort by multiple columns, the cursor must encode all sort column values: WHERE (CreatedAt < @ts) OR (CreatedAt = @ts AND Id < @id). This gets messy with 3+ sort columns.
  • No backward navigation without storing history — to go "back", you need to store the cursor stack client-side or reverse the query direction.

For most consumer-facing infinite-scroll UIs these limitations are fine. For admin tables with numbered pages and random access, OFFSET-FETCH is simpler despite its scale limitations.
followUpAnswers.How do you get total count efficiently w:
Getting an exact total row count for pagination is surprisingly expensive on large tables because it requires a full scan. Options:

  • Exact COUNT with same WHERE clause — execute the count query in parallel with the page query. Works fine for small-to-medium tables (<1M rows). Use a separate query, not a subquery inside the page query.
-- Page query
SELECT Id, Title FROM Articles
WHERE CategoryId = @catId
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC
OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 20 ROWS ONLY;

-- Count query (run in parallel)
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Articles WHERE CategoryId = @catId;
  • Approximate count using sys.dm_db_partition_stats — for total table count (no WHERE filter), this is instant:
    SELECT SUM(row_count) FROM sys.dm_db_partition_stats WHERE object_id = OBJECT_ID('Articles') AND index_id IN (0,1);
  • Cache the count — compute the total count periodically (e.g., every 60 seconds) and serve the cached value. Users see a slightly stale count, which is usually acceptable.
  • Accept approximate counts — many UIs show "100+ results" rather than exact counts. Implement a cap: if count > 1000, just return "1000+" without counting further.
  • Filtered indexes — a filtered index on the WHERE condition can make the count query much faster by storing only matching rows.

My approach: for end-user search results, cache the count for 30 seconds. For admin reports with exact requirements, run the count query with a covering index and accept the cost.

questions.tip: Avoid COUNT(*) for total pages on large tables — it's a full scan. Consider caching the count, accepting approximate counts, or using filtered indexes if total count is essential.

questions.question: What is the difference between a temp table and a table variable?
questions.answer:
Both store temporary data during a query session but they behave differently:

Feature#TempTable@TableVariable
ScopeSession / stored procBatch / current scope
Transaction-awareYes (rolls back)No (survives rollback)
StatisticsYes (optimizer uses real stats)No (assumes 1 row)
IndexesFull index supportOnly PK/UNIQUE
Tempdb usageAlways in tempdbIn tempdb for large sets, memory for small
Best forLarger result sets, complex processingSmall result sets (<100 rows), simple passing of data

Critical insight: Table variables tell the optimizer they have exactly 1 row (hardcoded estimate). If you put 50,000 rows in a table variable and join on it, the optimizer picks a catastrophically bad plan. For anything non-trivial, use a temp table so the optimizer gets real statistics.
followUps: What is tempdb? Why is it a bottleneck?
What is a table-valued parameter?

followUpAnswers.What is tempdb? Why is it a bottleneck?:
tempdb is a shared system database in SQL Server that's recreated from scratch every time SQL Server restarts. All temporary objects go here: temp tables, table variables (for large sets), sort spills, hash join spills, row versioning (SNAPSHOT isolation version store), Service Broker, and more.

Why tempdb becomes a bottleneck:

  • Contention on system pages — tempdb has special allocation pages (PFS, GAM, SGAM) that all concurrent object creations contend on. Under heavy concurrency, these pages become a serialization point.
  • Single-file default — by default tempdb has one data file. Multiple cores trying to allocate objects simultaneously queue up. Fix: create multiple tempdb data files (one per logical core, up to 8).
  • Version store growth — SNAPSHOT isolation stores row versions in tempdb. Under heavy OLTP with long-running transactions, the version store can grow to tens of GBs.
  • Sort and hash spills — when sorts or hash joins run out of granted memory, they spill to tempdb. Poor memory estimates from stale statistics cause frequent spills.
-- Recommended: 4-8 equal-sized tempdb data files
-- Configure in SQL Server Management Studio or:
ALTER DATABASE tempdb ADD FILE (NAME = tempdev2, FILENAME = 'D:\tempdb2.ndf', SIZE = 1024MB);

-- Check tempdb space usage
SELECT * FROM sys.dm_db_file_space_usage WHERE database_id = 2;
followUpAnswers.What is a table-valued parameter?:
A table-valued parameter (TVP) allows you to pass an entire table of data as a parameter to a stored procedure or function. It's the clean solution for passing multiple rows in a single call — replacing comma-separated strings or XML hacks.

-- Step 1: Create a user-defined table type
CREATE TYPE dbo.InvoiceIdList AS TABLE (InvoiceId INT NOT NULL);

-- Step 2: Create stored procedure accepting the TVP
CREATE PROCEDURE dbo.BulkUpdateInvoiceStatus
    @InvoiceIds dbo.InvoiceIdList READONLY,
    @NewStatus NVARCHAR(50)
AS
BEGIN
    UPDATE i SET Status = @NewStatus
    FROM Invoices i
    INNER JOIN @InvoiceIds ids ON i.Id = ids.InvoiceId;
END;
// C# usage with Dapper
var dt = new DataTable();
dt.Columns.Add("InvoiceId", typeof(int));
foreach (var id in invoiceIds) dt.Rows.Add(id);

await _db.ExecuteAsync(
    "dbo.BulkUpdateInvoiceStatus",
    new {
        InvoiceIds = dt.AsTableValuedParameter("dbo.InvoiceIdList"),
        NewStatus = "Paid"
    },
    commandType: CommandType.StoredProcedure);

TVPs vs alternatives:

  • TVP vs comma-separated: TVP is strongly typed and doesn't require string parsing.
  • TVP vs XML: TVP is faster and simpler to work with.
  • TVP vs temp table: TVP is scoped to the procedure call, no cleanup needed. Temp tables persist for the session.

questions.tip: When joining a large table variable, the optimizer assumes 1 row and picks nested loops. This causes performance disasters. Always use #TempTable for intermediate result sets that will be joined.

questions.question: What is SQL injection and how do you prevent it?
questions.answer:
SQL injection is when attacker-controlled input is interpreted as SQL commands instead of data. Classic example:

-- Vulnerable: string concatenation
var query = $"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = '{userInput}'";
-- Input: ' OR 1=1 --
-- Resulting SQL: SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = '' OR 1=1 --'
-- Returns ALL users!

Prevention — always use parameterized queries:

// Dapper (offline invoice project)
var user = await _db.QueryFirstOrDefaultAsync<User>(
    "SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = @name",
    new { name = userInput });  // @name is a parameter, never concatenated

// EF Core — always parameterized automatically
var user = await _db.Users
    .Where(u => u.Name == userInput)  // LINQ translates to @p0 parameter
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync();

// Raw SQL in EF Core — use FromSqlInterpolated NOT FromSqlRaw with concat
var users = _db.Users.FromSqlInterpolated($"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = {userInput}");

I've never had an injection vulnerability because I've never concatenated user input into SQL — always parameters or EF Core LINQ.
followUps: What is stored procedure injection?
How does parameterization prevent injection at the protocol level?

followUpAnswers.What is stored procedure injection?:
A common misconception is that stored procedures automatically prevent SQL injection. They don't — if the stored procedure uses dynamic SQL internally with string concatenation, it's still vulnerable.

-- VULNERABLE stored procedure: dynamic SQL with concatenation
CREATE PROCEDURE SearchInvoices @SearchTerm NVARCHAR(100)
AS
BEGIN
    DECLARE @sql NVARCHAR(500) =
        N'SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE Description LIKE ''%' + @SearchTerm + N'%''';
    EXEC(@sql);
END;

-- Attack: @SearchTerm = "'; DROP TABLE Invoices; --"
-- Resulting SQL: SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE Description LIKE '%'; DROP TABLE Invoices; --%'
-- SAFE: use sp_executesql with parameters
CREATE PROCEDURE SearchInvoices @SearchTerm NVARCHAR(100)
AS
BEGIN
    DECLARE @sql NVARCHAR(500) =
        N'SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE Description LIKE @pattern';
    DECLARE @pattern NVARCHAR(102) = N'%' + @SearchTerm + N'%';
    EXEC sp_executesql @sql, N'@pattern NVARCHAR(102)', @pattern = @pattern;
END;

Calling a stored procedure with parameters is safe — the parameter values are sent separately from the SQL text via the TDS protocol and are never interpreted as SQL. But if the stored procedure internally concatenates those parameters into a dynamic SQL string, the injection risk moves inside the procedure. Always use sp_executesql with parameters for any dynamic SQL, even inside stored procedures.
followUpAnswers.How does parameterization prevent inject:
Understanding why parameters prevent injection requires looking at how SQL Server processes the query:

String concatenation (vulnerable):

  • The entire SQL string — including user input — is sent to SQL Server as a single text blob.
  • SQL Server's parser reads it as one unified SQL statement. It cannot distinguish between SQL keywords you wrote and data the user provided.
  • A clever user can craft input that closes your string and appends new SQL commands.

Parameterized query (safe):

  • The SQL text and parameter values are sent as separate components in the TDS (Tabular Data Stream) protocol — the binary protocol between the .NET SqlClient and SQL Server.
  • The SQL text has placeholders (@name). SQL Server parses and compiles this template first, then separately receives the parameter values.
  • Parameter values are treated as pure data during execution — they're bound to the query as typed values, never re-parsed as SQL text.
  • A parameter value of ' OR 1=1 -- is passed as the literal string data ' OR 1=1 --, not as SQL syntax. SQL Server never re-parses it.
// What actually travels over the wire for a parameterized query:
// RPC call: sp_executesql
//   @stmt = N'SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = @name'
//   @params = N'@name nvarchar(50)'
//   @name = N''' OR 1=1 --'   ← treated as string data, never as SQL

This is also why parameterized queries enable plan cache reuse — the SQL template is stable, only the data values differ.

questions.tip: EF Core's FromSqlRaw is dangerous if used with string concatenation. Use FromSqlInterpolated (which safely parameterizes interpolated strings) or FromSqlRaw with explicit parameters only.

questions.question: What is the difference between primary key and unique key?
questions.answer:
Both enforce uniqueness but they differ in several important ways:

  • Primary Key (PK): Cannot be NULL. Only one per table. SQL Server automatically creates a clustered index on it (by default). Uniquely identifies each row — it's the main entity identifier.
  • Unique Key (Unique Constraint): Allows one NULL (because NULL != NULL in SQL). Multiple unique constraints allowed per table. Creates a non-clustered index by default. Used for alternate keys — business keys that must be unique but aren't the main identifier.

Example: in the Clients table, Id (INT IDENTITY) is the PK. Email has a UNIQUE constraint — it must be unique per client but it's not the clustered index because it's variable length and changes occasionally (people update emails).
followUps: Can a primary key be a composite key?
Why is a sequential integer usually preferred over a GUID for PK?

followUpAnswers.Can a primary key be a composite key?:
Yes. A composite primary key consists of two or more columns whose combined values uniquely identify each row. Neither column alone needs to be unique — the combination must be.

-- Composite PK: StudentId + CourseId together are unique
CREATE TABLE Enrollments (
    StudentId INT NOT NULL,
    CourseId  INT NOT NULL,
    EnrolledAt DATETIME2 NOT NULL,
    Grade CHAR(1) NULL,
    CONSTRAINT PK_Enrollments PRIMARY KEY (StudentId, CourseId)
);

-- EF Core composite PK via Fluent API
modelBuilder.Entity()
    .HasKey(e => new { e.StudentId, e.CourseId });

When composite PKs make sense:

  • Many-to-many junction tables (Enrollments, OrderItems, UserRoles)
  • Historical tables where the same entity can appear in multiple time periods: (EntityId, ValidFrom)
  • When there's a natural compound key with no better surrogate

Downsides of composite PKs:

  • All foreign keys referencing this table must include all PK columns — FKs become composite too, increasing join complexity.
  • EF Core works better with single-column PKs for Find(), lazy loading, and navigation properties.
  • Most developers prefer a surrogate INT IDENTITY PK on junction tables plus a UNIQUE constraint on the composite business key.
followUpAnswers.Why is a sequential integer usually pref:
Both work as primary keys but sequential integers have significant advantages in SQL Server:

AspectINT IDENTITY (sequential)GUID (newid())
Storage4 bytes16 bytes
Clustered index fragmentationNear-zero (always appends)High (random insertions)
Join performanceFast (small key, fits in memory)Slower (larger key, wider index)
ReadabilityHuman-readable (42, 1337)Not readable (f47ac10b-...)
Global uniquenessOnly within one DB/tableGlobally unique across all systems
Merge/sync across DBsConflicts on mergeNo conflicts
Security (enumerable?)IDs are guessableNot guessable

Choose GUIDs when: you need to generate IDs client-side before inserting (mobile offline sync), merge data from multiple databases, or want non-guessable IDs in URLs.

Best of both worlds: Use NEWSEQUENTIALID() for the clustered PK (sequential GUID, no fragmentation) and expose a separate non-guessable UUID in your API layer if security requires it.

In the CSInvoice project, migrating from GUID to INT IDENTITY PKs reduced the clustered index size by ~75% and eliminated the fragmentation problem entirely.

questions.tip: NULLs in unique constraints: Most databases allow multiple NULLs in a unique column because NULL != NULL. SQL Server is consistent with this. If you want to prevent multiple NULLs, add a filtered unique index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... WHERE Email IS NOT NULL.

questions.question: What is database partitioning in SQL Server?
questions.answer:
Table partitioning splits a large table into smaller physical storage units (partitions) based on a partition function and column value. From the application's perspective the table is still one logical table — SQL is unchanged.

Common strategy: partition by date (e.g., one partition per month). Old partitions rarely change and can be compressed or archived. New data hits only the current partition.

-- Partition function: split by year
CREATE PARTITION FUNCTION pfInvoiceYear (INT)
AS RANGE RIGHT FOR VALUES (2022, 2023, 2024, 2025);

-- Partition scheme maps partitions to filegroups
CREATE PARTITION SCHEME psInvoiceYear
AS PARTITION pfInvoiceYear ALL TO ([PRIMARY]);

-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE Invoices (
  Id INT, InvoiceYear INT, Amount DECIMAL(18,2)
) ON psInvoiceYear(InvoiceYear);

Benefits: Partition elimination (queries filtered on partition column skip non-relevant partitions), faster archiving (partition switch = metadata operation, instant), better maintenance (rebuild index per partition).
followUps: What is partition elimination?
How is partitioning different from sharding?

followUpAnswers.What is partition elimination?:
Partition elimination is when SQL Server's query optimizer determines that certain partitions cannot contain rows matching the query's WHERE clause and skips them entirely — reading only the relevant partitions.

-- Table partitioned by InvoiceYear
-- Partitions: 2022 | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 | future

-- Query with partition key in WHERE: SQL Server reads ONLY the 2024 partition
SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE InvoiceYear = 2024;

-- Without partition elimination: scans all partitions
SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE Amount > 1000;  -- no partition key filter

-- Verify elimination in execution plan: look for "Actual Partition Count"
-- vs total partition count — if they differ, elimination is working
SELECT * FROM sys.dm_exec_query_plan(...) ...  -- check plan XML for SeekPredicates

Requirements for elimination to work:

  • The WHERE clause must filter on the partition column (or a superset).
  • The filter value must be a constant or a parameter that's known at compile time.
  • Applying a function to the partition column (YEAR(InvoiceDate) when partitioned on raw date) can prevent elimination — store the partition key as a computed column or separate column.

Partition elimination is the primary performance benefit of partitioning. Without it, a partitioned table can actually be slower than a non-partitioned one (extra metadata overhead).
followUpAnswers.How is partitioning different from shard:
These are often confused but operate at completely different layers:

AspectTable PartitioningSharding
LocationWithin a single SQL Server instanceAcross multiple separate server instances
Application visibilityCompletely transparent — same query, same table nameApplication must route queries to the correct shard DB
TransactionsNormal local transactionsDistributed transactions (complex) or eventual consistency
Cross-partition joinsNormal SQL joins work fineNo SQL joins across shards — must be done in application code
PurposeManageability, archiving, partition eliminationHorizontal scale-out beyond single server capacity
ComplexityModerate (setup in SQL Server)Very high (application routing, distributed ops)

When to use which:

  • Partitioning — data has a natural time or range dimension (invoices by year, logs by month). Table is large (>100GB+). You want to archive old data cheaply. All data still fits on one server.
  • Sharding — you've outgrown a single server. Writes are the bottleneck. You need true horizontal scalability. Accept significantly higher operational and application complexity.

questions.tip: Partitioning vs sharding: Partitioning splits data across filegroups within a single SQL Server instance. Sharding splits data across multiple server instances. Partitioning is transparent to the application; sharding requires application-level routing.

questions.question: What is a composite index and when should you use it?
questions.answer:
A composite (multi-column) index includes more than one column. The order of columns in the index definition matters critically:

  • SQL Server can use a composite index if the query filters by the leading column(s).
  • Filtering on column 2 alone (skipping column 1) causes an index scan, not seek — effectively ignoring the index.
-- Index on (ClientId, Status, InvoiceDate)
CREATE INDEX IX_Invoices_Composite ON Invoices (ClientId, Status, InvoiceDate);

-- Uses index efficiently (leading columns present)
WHERE ClientId = 5 AND Status = 'Pending'

-- Also uses index (leading column present)
WHERE ClientId = 5

-- Does NOT use index efficiently (skips ClientId)
WHERE Status = 'Pending'

Design rule — selectivity first: Put the most selective column first (fewest distinct values per row, e.g., ClientId). But also consider your most common query patterns — sometimes you put a less selective equality column first so range scans happen on the second column.

I used a composite index on (ClientId, DueDate) in the PaymentReminder service — the nightly job queried exactly that pattern every time.
followUps: What is the rule of thumb for column order in composite indexes?
What is an index with INCLUDE columns?

followUpAnswers.What is the rule of thumb for column ord:
The canonical rule: Equality predicates first, then range predicates, then includes.

  • Equality columns first — columns used with = or IN in the most selective order. SQL Server can seek directly to the matching portion.
  • Range column last in the key — the column used with >, <, BETWEEN, or LIKE 'prefix%'. Ranges work best as the last key column because once SQL Server starts a range scan it can't also seek on subsequent columns.
  • INCLUDE columns — columns needed by SELECT but not filtered on. Keep out of the key, put in INCLUDE.
-- Query: WHERE ClientId = @id AND Status = 'Pending' AND DueDate > @date
-- ORDER BY DueDate
-- SELECT ..., Amount, InvoiceNumber

-- Optimal index:
CREATE INDEX IX_Optimal ON Invoices
    (ClientId, Status, DueDate)  -- equality, equality, range
INCLUDE (Amount, InvoiceNumber);  -- SELECT-only columns

-- This index supports:
-- 1. Seek on ClientId = @id AND Status = 'Pending'
-- 2. Range scan on DueDate > @date within that partition
-- 3. Sorted output (eliminates Sort operator)
-- 4. Covers Amount and InvoiceNumber (no key lookup)

Secondary consideration — selectivity: When choosing between two equality columns, put the more selective one first (e.g., ClientId with 10,000 distinct values before Status with 3 distinct values). This reduces the rows SQL Server examines after the first key comparison.
followUpAnswers.What is an index with INCLUDE columns?:
The INCLUDE clause in a CREATE INDEX statement adds columns to the leaf level of the non-clustered index without including them in the sort key structure. This creates a covering index without the overhead of extra sort key columns.

-- Non-covering: seek finds rows, then key lookup fetches Amount and InvoiceDate
CREATE INDEX IX_Basic ON Invoices (ClientId, Status);

-- Covering with INCLUDE: no key lookup needed
CREATE INDEX IX_Covering ON Invoices (ClientId, Status)
INCLUDE (Amount, InvoiceDate, InvoiceNumber);

-- Query that benefits from the covering index:
SELECT Amount, InvoiceDate, InvoiceNumber  -- all in index
FROM Invoices
WHERE ClientId = 42 AND Status = 'Pending'; -- seek on key columns

Key vs INCLUDE — what's the difference?

  • Key columns affect the B-tree sort order and enable seeks and range navigation.
  • INCLUDE columns are only at the leaf level (the data pages of the index). They don't affect sorting or seeking.
  • Columns in INCLUDE don't count against the 16-column / 900-byte key column limits (SQL Server 2005+ allows up to 1,023 INCLUDE columns).

When to add INCLUDE: When a query frequently hits a key lookup after your index seek (visible in the execution plan as a "Key Lookup" operator with significant cost), add the columns being looked up to your index's INCLUDE list. Each key lookup eliminated is a random I/O saved.

questions.tip: Think of composite indexes like a phone book: sorted by last name then first name. You can look up by last name alone (efficient), but looking up by first name alone means reading the whole book.

questions.question: What are aggregate functions in SQL? Give examples.
questions.answer:
Aggregate functions compute a single result from a set of rows. They're used with GROUP BY or across the entire table.

  • COUNT(*) — total row count. COUNT(column) ignores NULLs.
  • SUM(column) — total sum. NULLs ignored.
  • AVG(column) — average. NULLs ignored (denominator = non-null count, not total rows).
  • MIN(column) / MAX(column) — min/max value.
  • STRING_AGG(column, separator) — concatenate string values (SQL Server 2017+).
SELECT
  ClientId,
  COUNT(*) AS InvoiceCount,
  SUM(Amount) AS TotalBilled,
  AVG(Amount) AS AvgInvoice,
  MAX(DueDate) AS LatestDue,
  STRING_AGG(Status, ', ') AS AllStatuses
FROM Invoices
GROUP BY ClientId
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;

NULL trap: AVG(Amount) divides by the count of non-null values. If 3 out of 10 rows have NULL Amount, AVG divides by 7, not 10 — potentially misleading.
followUps: How does COUNT(*) differ from COUNT(1) and COUNT(column)?
What is GROUPING SETS?

followUpAnswers.How does COUNT(*) differ from COUNT(1) a:
This is a classic interview question. The answer has changed with modern SQL Server:

  • COUNT(*) — counts all rows in the group, including rows with NULLs in any column. It counts the row, not any particular column's value.
  • COUNT(1) — historically thought to be faster than COUNT(*) because it counts a constant (1) rather than "looking at all columns". In modern SQL Server this is a myth — the optimizer generates identical execution plans for COUNT(*) and COUNT(1). Use COUNT(*) for clarity.
  • COUNT(column) — counts only rows where that specific column is NOT NULL. This gives a different (potentially smaller) result than COUNT(*).
-- Table: 10 rows, 3 have NULL in Amount column
SELECT
    COUNT(*)      AS TotalRows,    -- 10
    COUNT(1)      AS AlsoTotalRows, -- 10 (same as COUNT(*))
    COUNT(Amount) AS NonNullAmount  -- 7 (excludes 3 NULLs)
FROM Invoices;

-- Practical use case: count how many invoices have an amount vs how many total
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS TotalInvoices,
    COUNT(Amount) AS InvoicesWithAmount,
    COUNT(*) - COUNT(Amount) AS InvoicesMissingAmount
FROM Invoices;

Rule: always use COUNT(*) when you want total rows. Use COUNT(column) specifically when you want to count non-null occurrences of a particular value.
followUpAnswers.What is GROUPING SETS?:
GROUPING SETS allows you to generate multiple GROUP BY levels in a single query — equivalent to multiple GROUP BY queries combined with UNION ALL, but more efficient (single pass over the data).

-- Without GROUPING SETS: 3 separate queries, 3 table scans
SELECT Region, Category, SUM(Sales) FROM Orders GROUP BY Region, Category
UNION ALL
SELECT Region, NULL, SUM(Sales) FROM Orders GROUP BY Region
UNION ALL
SELECT NULL, NULL, SUM(Sales) FROM Orders;

-- With GROUPING SETS: one query, one table scan
SELECT Region, Category, SUM(Sales) AS TotalSales
FROM Orders
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
    (Region, Category),  -- subtotals per region+category
    (Region),            -- subtotals per region
    ()                   -- grand total
);

Related shortcuts:

  • ROLLUP — automatically generates a hierarchy of groupings from the most detailed to the grand total. GROUP BY ROLLUP(Region, Category) generates: (Region, Category), (Region), ().
  • CUBE — generates all possible combinations. GROUP BY CUBE(Region, Category) generates: (Region, Category), (Region), (Category), ().
  • GROUPING(column) function — returns 1 if the column is part of a summary row (NULL due to ROLLUP/CUBE), 0 otherwise. Use to distinguish between a real NULL and an aggregation NULL.

GROUPING SETS is excellent for building reporting queries that need subtotals and grand totals — common in financial dashboards and pivot reports.

questions.tip: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column): COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs. COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values in that column. These can give different numbers on the same table — always use COUNT(*) when you want total rows.

questions.question: Explain the SQL Server query execution pipeline.
questions.answer:
When you submit a query to SQL Server, it goes through these stages:

  1. Parsing — syntax check, builds a parse tree. Fails here if SQL is malformed.
  2. Binding (Algebrisation) — resolves object names (table names, column names) against the system catalog. Verifies the tables/columns exist.
  3. Query Optimisation — the optimizer considers multiple execution plans (join order, index choice, join type) and picks the lowest-cost one based on statistics. This is the most expensive step — plans are cached to avoid repeating it.
  4. Plan Cache Check — before optimising, SQL Server checks if a plan for this query already exists in the plan cache (matching parameter signature). If yes, skips optimization and reuses the plan.
  5. Execution — the execution engine runs the chosen plan, interacting with the buffer pool (memory) and reading from disk if needed (physical I/O).

Buffer pool (memory): SQL Server caches data pages in memory. Repeated queries on the same data avoid disk I/O. This is why "warming up" a SQL Server after restart helps — the buffer pool fills over time.
followUps: What is plan cache pollution?
What is the difference between logical and physical reads?

followUpAnswers.What is plan cache pollution?:
Plan cache pollution occurs when the plan cache fills up with thousands of nearly identical single-use query plans, consuming memory that could be used for data pages and forcing constant recompilation.

Primary cause: ad-hoc queries with literal values

-- Each of these generates a SEPARATE cache entry:
SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = 1;
SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = 2;
SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = 3;
-- 3 cache entries, each used once

-- Parameterized: ONE cache entry, reused for all ClientId values
SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = @clientId;

Solutions:

  • Parameterize all queries — the root fix. Always use parameters, never literals in queries.
  • Optimize for ad-hoc workloads — SQL Server setting that stores only a stub (not the full plan) on first execution. Full plan stored only on second execution of the same query. Dramatically reduces cache waste from single-use queries.
-- Enable optimize for ad-hoc workloads
EXEC sp_configure 'optimize for ad hoc workloads', 1;
RECONFIGURE;

-- Check plan cache size and quality
SELECT
    SUM(size_in_bytes) / 1024 / 1024 AS CacheSizeMB,
    COUNT(*) AS PlanCount,
    SUM(CASE WHEN usecounts = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS SingleUsePlans
FROM sys.dm_exec_cached_plans
WHERE objtype = 'Adhoc';

If >50% of ad-hoc plans are single-use, you have a pollution problem. Enable the ad-hoc optimization setting as a first step — it's a zero-cost, no-restart change.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between logical a:
These terms come from SQL Server's storage architecture where data is read from either memory (buffer pool) or disk:

  • Logical reads — the number of 8KB data pages read from the buffer pool (memory cache). Fast — microseconds. Every query has logical reads.
  • Physical reads — the number of 8KB data pages read from disk because they weren't in the buffer pool. Slow — milliseconds. High physical reads = cold cache or working set exceeds memory.
  • Read-ahead reads — pages SQL Server pre-fetched from disk in anticipation of need (speculative prefetch). Counted separately but still involve disk I/O.
-- Measure reads for a query
SET STATISTICS IO ON;

SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = 42;

-- Output example:
-- Table 'Invoices'. Scan count 1, logical reads 847, physical reads 0, read-ahead reads 0.

SET STATISTICS IO OFF;

Practical interpretation:

  • High logical reads with zero physical reads = data is warm in cache. Query is I/O-efficient from a disk perspective, but high logical reads still mean more CPU work. Reduce logical reads by adding better indexes.
  • High physical reads = cold cache or data doesn't fit in memory. First occurrence after restart is expected. Persisting high physical reads in steady state = memory pressure or a missing index causing large scans.
  • Goal: minimize logical reads through better indexes; physical reads will follow once the buffer pool warms up.

questions.tip: Logical reads vs physical reads: Logical = reads from buffer pool (memory, fast). Physical = reads from disk (slow). SET STATISTICS IO ON shows both. Tuning aims to reduce physical reads by ensuring the working set fits in memory.

questions.question: What is the difference between CHAR, VARCHAR, and NVARCHAR?
questions.answer:
String data type choice affects storage, performance, and internationalization:

  • CHAR(n) — fixed-length, always uses n bytes (right-padded with spaces). Best for fixed-length codes (country codes, status flags like 'Y'/'N'). Comparison is fast because length is always known.
  • VARCHAR(n) — variable-length, uses actual length + 2 bytes overhead. Best for variable-length ASCII/Latin text. More storage-efficient than CHAR for variable content.
  • NVARCHAR(n) — variable-length Unicode (UTF-16). Uses 2 bytes per character. Required for multilingual content (names in Hindi, Chinese, Arabic, emoji). NVARCHAR(MAX) can hold up to 2GB.

Performance: CHAR is slightly faster for comparison (fixed length). VARCHAR and NVARCHAR have similar performance. NVARCHAR uses 2x the storage of VARCHAR for the same content.

Rule: Use NVARCHAR for user-facing text (names, descriptions, messages). Use VARCHAR for internal codes/identifiers where Unicode isn't needed. Never use NVARCHAR just to be safe — the storage cost adds up on large tables.
followUps: When would you use VARCHAR(MAX)?
What is the difference between N'' and '' string literals?

followUpAnswers.When would you use VARCHAR(MAX)?:
VARCHAR(MAX) and NVARCHAR(MAX) store up to 2GB of character data. Use them when content genuinely can't be bounded at design time — but understand the trade-offs:

When to use VARCHAR(MAX) / NVARCHAR(MAX):

  • Email/document body text (no practical size limit)
  • JSON or XML blobs stored in a column
  • Markdown or HTML content (blog posts, descriptions, notes)
  • Audit log details or error messages that may be arbitrarily long

Performance trade-offs:

  • Columns up to 8,000 bytes are stored inline in the data row. MAX columns over 8,000 bytes are stored off-row in LOB (Large Object) pages — requiring extra I/O to fetch.
  • Cannot be used as an index key column (non-clustered indexes support only up to 900 bytes per key).
  • Cannot be used in DISTINCT, GROUP BY, or ORDER BY without truncation.
  • String functions like LEN(), SUBSTRING() work on MAX columns but are slower on very large values.
-- Reasonable use: notes/description that could be long
CREATE TABLE Questions (
    Id INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    Title NVARCHAR(500) NOT NULL,       -- bounded
    BodyHtml NVARCHAR(MAX) NULL,         -- unbounded, stored off-row if large
    Tags NVARCHAR(200) NULL             -- bounded
);

Rule: use a bounded size (NVARCHAR(500), NVARCHAR(4000)) whenever you can justify the limit. Use MAX only when content is truly unbounded.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between N'' and ':
The N prefix marks a string literal as Unicode (NVARCHAR). Without N, the literal is treated as a non-Unicode VARCHAR in the server's default collation.

-- Without N: VARCHAR literal, interpreted in server default collation
SELECT 'Hello'          -- varchar
SELECT 'Héllo'          -- may corrupt 'é' if default collation doesn't support it

-- With N: NVARCHAR literal, full Unicode support
SELECT N'Hello'         -- nvarchar
SELECT N'नमस्ते'        -- works fine: Hindi text
SELECT N'こんにちは'     -- works fine: Japanese text

Why it matters — data corruption scenario:

-- Column is NVARCHAR(100), but you insert without N prefix:
INSERT INTO Users (Name) VALUES ('Müller');  -- may lose the 'ü' character
INSERT INTO Users (Name) VALUES (N'Müller'); -- correct: preserves 'ü'

-- Filtering also breaks without N prefix on NVARCHAR columns:
WHERE Name = 'Müller'   -- implicit conversion, may not match
WHERE Name = N'Müller'  -- correct comparison

In application code: parameterized queries (EF Core, Dapper, SqlCommand with parameters) handle this automatically — the SqlClient driver sends string parameters as Unicode by default. The N-prefix issue only applies when writing raw SQL literals in scripts, migrations, or seed data files. Always use the N prefix in any SQL script that touches NVARCHAR columns.

questions.tip: Always prefix Unicode string literals with N: N'Abdul' not 'Abdul'. Without N, SQL Server uses the default collation (may corrupt non-ASCII characters). In parameterized queries this is handled automatically.
